Headline 35-NFE result also bundles EDM TRAINING improvements; the training-free transferable piece is the Heun solver + discretization, whose protein speedup must be benchmarked, not assumed.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: PARTIAL — see detailed field above; solver+discretization yes, churn needs sigma-space->t-space derivation.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: YES. Nothing in EDM is image-specific; the solver and schedule act on the coordinate ODE/SDE regardless of the data being 3D CA-only coordinates. The Heun update and EDM schedule operate on Proteina's vector-field integration loop directly. Proteina already exposes schedule_mode='edm', confirming the discretization is usable on CA-only backbones.
verdict.implementation_cost: LOW for the deterministic-ODE branch: swap Euler->Heun in r3n_fm.py step_euler()/full_simulation() (r3n_fm.py:269-351, 418-576); the 'edm' schedule already exists in get_schedule() (r3n_fm.py:639-693), so the discretization is free. MODERATE for porting the stochastic churn to Proteina's 'sc' SDE: requires deriving noise add/remove amounts in FM t-space via the velocity<->score change of variables (cannot copy S_churn formula literally). No training, no new dependencies. Code license is CC-BY-NC-SA (non-commercial) — fine for research, reimplement the ~20-line sampler rather than vendoring if licensing matters.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Training-free. Credible ~2x: Heun's 2nd-order accuracy plausibly halves NFE at matched quality on the DETERMINISTIC ODE (~200->~100), consistent with EDM's image-domain result, but the exact crossover is model/data-specific and MUST be measured on Proteina, not assumed. The headline 35-NFE/FID-1.79 is a fully-trained-EDM image result that bundles training improvements — not a pure training-free transfer claim. Important: Proteina's default sampler is the STOCHASTIC 'sc' SDE; Heun's clean 2x applies to the ODE, so the speedup is most credible if switching the baseline to ODE ('vf', the plan's open Q4). Accelerating the SDE as-is via churn is the harder, derivation-dependent path with less certain gain.
verdict.quality_cost: Low if applied to the deterministic ODE branch with adequate steps: Heun matches Euler quality at roughly half the NFE, so the ~2x cut is near-lossless for ODE sampling. Risks specific to this project: (1) the quality-vs-NFE crossover must be benchmarked on the fold/topology gate (GearNet/USalign), since the image-FID crossover does not guarantee the protein crossover; (2) if the team keeps the stochastic 'sc' SDE (current default), a mis-mapped churn port can ADD noise incorrectly and DEGRADE quality — the t-space derivation must be validated against a self-consistency check vs the 200-step reference. No quality cost is free without local measurement.

verifiedPapers.title: Leveraging Previous Steps: A Training-free Fast Solver for Flow Diffusion (FlowSolver)
verifiedPapers.category: training-free fast solver designed FOR flow matching (multistep / polynomial Taylor)
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Adapts the DPM-Solver/DEIS multistep idea natively to FLOW-MATCHING velocity-prediction models: caches previous solver steps and uses polynomial interpolation to approximate the high-order Taylor derivatives of the velocity field, raising solver order without extra NFE or retraining. Built specifically for the linear/flow ODE rather than the score ODE.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Most directly transferable in principle — it already assumes a velocity-field FM model (exactly Proteina's parameterization), so no noise/score reparameterization needed. Demonstrated only on image FM models; CA-only protein application untested but the math carries over directly.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: FID-30K 13.79->6.75 (CIFAR-10) and 46.64->19.49 (LSUN-Church) at NFE=10 vs baseline Euler at equal NFE.
verifiedPapers.public_code_url: not located (no public repo found as of search)
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Medium. Conceptually simple (cache + polynomial extrapolation of velocity) and matches our model's native parameterization, but no released code found, so likely a from-paper reimplementation.
verifiedPapers.notes: Highest conceptual fit: a training-free multistep solver written for velocity-prediction FM ODEs, so it sidesteps the score/diffusion reparameterization caveat that DPM-Solver/DEIS/UniPC carry. Only addresses the deterministic ODE; combine with EDM-churn or Restart to retain Proteina's stochastic sampler. Reimplementation needed.

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL, training-free, flow-matching-native fast solver (arXiv 2411.07627) whose claimed numbers check out. It is a legitimate but second-tier contribution: validated only on images and only on the deterministic ODE, with no public code and weak baselines (no DPM-Solver/UniPC/DEIS comparison). For Proteina it is promising for accelerating the DETERMINISTIC ODE sampling path (plausible 4-10x NFE cut into the ~20-50 range), is cheap to implement as a drop-in multistep update with no retraining, but (a) gives nothing for Proteina's DEFAULT SDE/noise-injection sampler, (b) has zero protein/3D evidence, so fold-topology quality vs NFE must be measured empirically before trusting it for a topology-critical template-search pipeline.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: YES — and this is the paper's central selling point. It is derived specifically for the linear/flow velocity-prediction ODE (v_theta(x_t,t)), not adapted from the score ODE. Base models are all flow-matching/rectified-flow (Rectified Flow, SiT-XL, SD3). The Taylor + polynomial-interpolation multistep update applies directly to Proteina's velocity field. IMPORTANT LIMITATION: the derivation/experiments cover the DETERMINISTIC ODE only. Proteina's default is a stochastic SDE ('sc') sampler with g(t) noise injection; the paper offers nothing for the stochastic term, so it transfers cleanly only to Proteina's deterministic ODE-mode sampling (or you must drop noise injection). The method is path-agnostic in derivation but only validated on straight/linear-interpolation paths, which is exactly Proteina's OT/linear flow — so no path mismatch.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Plausibly yes, but UNTESTED on any non-image domain. The solver is a generic multistep ODE integrator operating on the model's velocity output; it is dimension/representation-agnostic, so it would mechanically apply to Proteina's CA-only coordinate velocity field. However there is ZERO evidence for molecular/point-cloud/3D/protein structure: no such experiments exist, and protein backbone generation is far more sensitive to fold/topology errors than image FID. Higher-order extrapolation can amplify error when the velocity field is stiff or near the data manifold (the authors themselves note approximation error limits how aggressively they can extrapolate, and that CFG-style guidance worsens it). For a topology-critical downstream template search, the high-order steps must be validated empirically (designability/scTM, fold/topology retention) before trusting them — do not assume image FID gains transfer to fold quality.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Credible regime: NFE roughly 7-10 with a several-x quality improvement OVER EULER at equal NFE (i.e., better samples at the same step budget, or equal quality at fewer steps). The CIFAR-10 13.79->6.6 and LSUN-Church 46.64->19.49 @NFE10, and 23.43->8.93 / 84.47->34.32 @NFE7 numbers are credible and self-consistent. For Proteina this most realistically means: from the current NFE=200 Euler default, a high-order flow multistep solver could plausibly target the ~20-50 NFE range for the ODE path (a 4-10x wall-clock cut) IF quality holds. The paper's own data does NOT support pushing below ~7 NFE without quality collapse, and its absolute quality is not competitive with DPM-Solver-v3/UniPC, so claims of extreme (NFE<=4) speedup are NOT credible from this work.
verdict.quality_cost: At equal NFE the method IMPROVES quality vs Euler (that is the whole result), so 'cost' is better framed as: how few steps can you take before fold quality degrades. Paper shows graceful degradation down to ~NFE7 on images and sharp degradation below that. Authors flag two quality risks directly relevant to proteins: (1) the model's own approximation error caps how much high-order extrapolation helps; (2) guidance/CFG increases approximation error. For Proteina specifically the unquantified risk is that high-order velocity extrapolation harms fold/topology more than it harms image FID — must be measured (designability, scTM/scRMSD, novelty, and downstream template-search hit rate) on a held-out set, NOT assumed.
verdict.implementation_cost: LOW-to-MODERATE, but you must implement it yourself (NO public code confirmed). It is a drop-in replacement for the Euler update in Proteina's ODE integration loop: maintain a small ring buffer of the last p velocity evaluations, compute finite-difference D_m terms, fit the polynomial-interpolation coefficients B_m, and apply the high-order update — a few dozen lines, no retraining, no new network. Effort items: (a) re-derive/port the coefficient formulas from the paper (no reference impl to copy); (b) it targets Proteina's deterministic ODE path only — to use it with the default SDE sampler you must either switch to ODE-mode or design how to combine it with the g(t) noise term (the paper gives no recipe, so this is non-trivial and would be an ungrounded design choice — flag to user); (c) validate fold/topology quality vs NFE empirically. Net: cheap to code, but the SDE-integration question and protein-quality validation are the real work.

verifiedPapers.title: Align Your Steps: Optimizing Sampling Schedules in Diffusion Models (AYS)
verifiedPapers.authors: Amirmojtaba Sabour, Sanja Fidler, Karsten Kreis (NVIDIA)
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Uses stochastic calculus (KL-divergence upper bound on the discretization error) to compute the OPTIMAL set of timesteps for a GIVEN solver, model, and dataset — replacing hand-crafted uniform/log/EDM schedules. Solver-specific and dataset-specific optimal schedules.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Directly relevant to our schedule_mode/schedule_p knobs: AYS is the principled way to set those discretization points instead of guessing among uniform/log/power/cos/edm. The KL-bound machinery applies to the FM ODE/SDE; would need to be derived/fit for the CA-only Proteina noise schedule, but no retraining.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Consistent quality gains at fixed low NFE (~10) across Euler/DPM/EDM samplers on CIFAR-10/FFHQ/ImageNet; text-to-image outputs preferred ~2x in user study at equal steps.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Medium. Precomputed schedules are dataset-specific; deriving an AYS schedule for our CA Proteina model requires running their optimization (a one-time offline cost), but inference is then free.
verifiedPapers.notes: Squarely targets the project's exposed knobs (schedule_mode, schedule_p) and the observation that naive uniform step-cutting degrades quality. Same NVIDIA group as Proteina, so conventions align. Best 'spend offline compute once, then sample cheaply' lever; complementary to (stackable with) any high-order solver above.

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and accurately described. "Align Your Steps" (AYS), arXiv 2404.14507, Sabour/Fidler/Kreis (NVIDIA), ICML 2024. Core idea confirmed: it optimizes the sampling TIMESTEP SCHEDULE (not the solver) by minimizing a KL-divergence Upper Bound (KLUB) between the true generative SDE and the linearized SDE that a given solver exactly solves, yielding solver-, model-, and dataset-specific optimal schedules that replace hand-crafted uniform/log/EDM heuristics. Claims of real benefit at low NFE, public schedules, and ComfyUI/diffusers integration all verified against the paper PDF, the official project page, and the third-party ecosystem. Two caveats on the claim wording: (a) "requires_training:false" is true only in the sense of no model retraining/distillation — the schedule optimization is itself a non-trivial one-time GPU precompute (trained model + ~8192 data samples + Monte-Carlo KLUB, <300 iters; e.g. ImageNet256 = 8 GPUs x 3-4h); (b) the "~2x preferred in user study" is loose — the actual video user study (42 participants) split 58/42 (~1.4x). For our flow-matching use case the decisive fact is favorable: the authors EXPLICITLY state (Conclusions/Future Work) that AYS is "not strictly limited to diffusion models" and transfers to flow matching and stochastic interpolants, because with a Gaussian noise prior these are reformulations of the same framework and always permit forming a generative SDE — the object AYS needs. BUT this is asserted as future work and NOT demonstrated; no FM/rectified-flow experiment exists, and only image schedules/code are released. Net: a credible, on-target lever for cutting Proteina NFE, but it needs a moderate from-scratch re-derivation/implementation of the KLUB for the linear-OT flow on CA coordinates; speedup is real but modest (~1.5x, larger only versus a badly-tuned EDM baseline).
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: YES, in principle, and uniquely well-grounded among schedule-optimization methods: the paper's Conclusions/Future Work section explicitly names flow matching (Lipman 2022, Esser 2024) and stochastic interpolants (Albergo 2023, Ma 2024) as compatible, stating that with a Gaussian noise prior these "correspond to re-formulations of the same underlying generation framework and always allow us to form a generative SDE, necessary for the application of AYS." AYS is formulated in noise-level/SDE space, not tied to VP or EDM parametrization specifically (it uses the general probability-flow ODE/SDE). HOWEVER: this transfer is stated as future work, NOT demonstrated — all experiments are diffusion/score models (EDM-based DMs, latent DMs, SD/SDXL/SVD). No rectified-flow or CFM experiment, and the released code/schedules are image-diffusion-specific. So transfer is theoretically sound but requires re-deriving the KLUB for Proteina's linear-OT flow drift and re-running the optimization; it is not plug-and-play for flow matching.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: YES in principle. The method is dimension-agnostic and operates on the per-timestep SDE discretization error, not on image-specific structure; it is demonstrated on 2D toy data (8-step SDE-DPM-Solver++), so low-dimensional / non-image / structured data is explicitly within scope. For Proteina (CA-only backbone, sequence-conditioned, linear-OT flow with Euler integration, default ~200 NFE, default stochastic SDE sampler): AYS directly targets exactly this setting (a stochastic SDE sampler whose timestep schedule is currently a hand-set knob among uniform/log/power/cos/edm). The KLUB is estimated by Monte-Carlo over a sample of data (here CA backbones) plus the trained network, which Proteina provides. Conditioning caveat: AYS notes classifier-free-guidance / conditional models make each guidance value effectively a different score model, but found one schedule generalizes across a reasonable guidance range — so a single sequence-conditioned schedule should be reusable.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Credible speedup ~1.5x at equal quality, not dramatic. Concrete paper numbers (ImageNet256, Stochastic DDIM): NFE=10 FID AYS 23.13 vs time-uniform 24.48 vs EDM 66.71; NFE=20 FID AYS 8.96 vs time-uniform 9.32 vs EDM 17.42. The large gap is only versus the poorly-tuned EDM schedule; versus a competently chosen time-uniform/log baseline the FID gain is modest (~1.05-1.3x). Headline self-reported speedup: a 20-step AYS schedule matches a 30-step default (SDE-DPM-Solver++(2M)) = 1.5x. Schedules transfer across solvers (a schedule optimized on one solver's KLUB generalizes to others) and upsample to higher NFE. Gains concentrate in the low-NFE regime and shrink as NFE grows or solver order rises. For Proteina's stated goal (cut 200 -> ~100 NFE without quality loss), AYS is plausibly sufficient to recover the quality naive halving loses, but expect roughly 1.3-2x effective NFE reduction at iso-quality, not more.
verdict.quality_cost: Low-to-negligible at iso-NFE if the schedule is correctly optimized: AYS is designed to IMPROVE quality at fixed NFE, and across image/video/2D benchmarks it matches or beats hand-crafted schedules "almost across the board." The risk is not quality regression from AYS itself but (a) a mis-derived KLUB for the flow-matching drift giving a suboptimal schedule that performs no better than a tuned log/uniform baseline, and (b) the well-tuned baselines already capturing most of the benefit, leaving little headroom. The paper also notes FID can behave non-monotonically at very low NFE without thresholding (higher-order solver instabilities) — for Proteina's fold/topology quality metric this needs empirical validation, not assumed. Net quality cost: expected near-zero at iso-NFE; the practical question is whether the realized speedup at iso-fold-quality justifies the build cost.
verdict.implementation_cost: Moderate, one-time. No public flow-matching implementation exists (released artifacts are precomputed image-diffusion schedules + diffusers/ComfyUI usage, NOT the FM optimization). Required work: (1) re-derive/instantiate the KLUB and its linearized-SDE for Proteina's linear-OT flow drift (the paper gives Algorithms 1-2 and the KLUB for sigma(t)=t, s(t)=1, which is close to the OT-FM form, so this is tractable, not from zero); (2) implement Monte-Carlo KLUB estimation calling the trained Proteina network on a subset (~8192) of CA backbones with time-based importance sampling; (3) run the schedule optimization (<300 iterations, coordinate-descent over a handful of timesteps; the paper used 4-8 GPUs x ~1.5-4h for image models — Proteina's smaller network/data makes this cheaper). The schedule is a per-model (and per-guidance-regime) precompute, then plug-and-play at inference by swapping the timestep array — zero inference-time overhead. Estimated effort: a focused implementation + tuning task, on the order of days, not weeks, given the algorithms are published.

verifiedPapers.title: Optimal Stepsize for Diffusion Sampling (OSS)
verifiedPapers.authors: Jianning Pei, Han Hu, Shuyang Gu
verifiedPapers.category: Optimal stepsize via dynamic programming distilled from reference trajectories
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Casts stepsize selection as recursive error minimization with optimal substructure and solves it with dynamic programming to distill a 'theoretically optimal' schedule from reference (teacher) trajectories. Unlike GITS's fixed local-cost matrix, OSS aims at the global discretization error. Resulting schedules are reported robust across architectures, ODE solvers, and noise schedules.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising. 'Distillation' here means distilling a SCHEDULE from reference trajectories, not retraining the base network — the base model is frozen. The DP/optimal-substructure formulation is solver- and noise-schedule-agnostic and explicitly claimed robust across noise schedules, so adapting to an OT flow-matching ODE is plausible: generate high-NFE reference CA trajectories with Proteina, then run the DP to extract a few-step schedule. Need to confirm the error metric used is meaningful in CA-coordinate space.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: ~10x acceleration in text-to-image while preserving 99.4% of GenEval performance; emphasizes very-low-NFE schedules.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Low-moderate. Generate reference trajectories once, run DP. Frozen base model.
verifiedPapers.notes: The current state-of-the-art 'optimal time-schedule search' for a fixed model, improving on GITS by targeting global rather than local error, with clean public code and claimed cross-noise-schedule robustness. Top candidate alongside GITS. The word 'distillation' can mislead — it is schedule distillation, NOT model distillation, so it stays training-free for our purposes.

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and one of the best-fitting accelerators for this project. "Optimal Stepsize for Diffusion Sampling" (OSS), arXiv 2503.21774, Mar 2025, official code at github.com/bebebe666/OptimalSteps. Casts stepsize selection as recursive global-error minimization with optimal substructure, solved by dynamic programming to distill a schedule from teacher trajectories; explicitly contrasts GITS's fixed local-cost matrix vs. OSS's global discretization error. Reported ~10x text-to-image (FLUX 100->10 steps) at 99.4% GenEval. Every claimed property checks out. Crucially for Proteina: the core update is x_j = x_i + v*(t_j - t_i) -- pure velocity/flow-matching, not EDM/score -- and the reference implementations (FLUX, Wan2.1) ARE flow-matching models. This is a NATIVE flow-matching method, not a transferred diffusion one.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Credible: ~10x on FLUX (100->10 steps) at 99.4% GenEval; 5x on Wan (100->20). For Proteina, a more conservative 2-4x (200 NFE -> ~50-100) is the safe target to claim before empirical validation on CA backbones, since the dramatic image numbers were measured against an image teacher with different error tolerance than a template-search quality bar. The DP finds a per-model schedule; OSS-ave (averaged over ~512 samples) gives one reusable schedule. Note the schedule search itself costs a one-time offline O(M*N^2) model-evaluation pass plus teacher-trajectory generation -- amortized over all future sampling.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: Yes, natively -- this is the standout point. The method's update equation is the linear flow-matching/v-prediction step x_j = x_i + v*(t_j-t_i), identical in form to Proteina's Euler FM-ODE integrator. The DP reparameterizes all frameworks into velocity space, so it does NOT assume score/EDM. Official examples are FLUX and Wan2.1, both flow-matching. One real gap: the paper's evidence and the x_j=x_i+v*dt formulation are for the DETERMINISTIC ODE. Proteina's default sampler is stochastic (SDE 'sc' with g(t) noise injection). OSS optimizes the ODE discretization; whether the distilled schedule preserves quality on the SDE/noise-injection path is unverified and is the main open risk -- mitigate by either applying OSS to ODE-mode sampling or re-deriving the search with the SDE update.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Plausibly yes, but unproven for this domain. Method is data/architecture-agnostic (operates only on the velocity field and trajectory endpoints in coordinate space), so CA-only backbone coordinates are just another tensor it integrates. No protein/molecular/3D experiments exist in the paper -- all results are image/video. Requires empirical validation: run the DP search on Proteina's own model and check the distilled low-NFE schedule against the downstream fold/topology (template-search) quality bar, not just coordinate L2.
verdict.quality_cost: Low-to-moderate, but must be measured on the project's metric. The paper minimizes endpoint L2 / SNR-weighted x0-MSE, which is a proxy for sample fidelity, not for fold/topology correctness that the template-search pipeline depends on. Image results show near-lossless quality at 10x; a 2-4x cut on Proteina is likely safe, but the schedule should be selected/validated against designability/topology metrics. Quality cost is also tied to how far you push NFE down and to the ODE-vs-SDE mismatch above.
verdict.implementation_cost: Low-to-moderate. (1) Generate teacher trajectories from Proteina at high NFE (e.g. 200). (2) Port search_OSS (provided, model-agnostic) to call Proteina's velocity network with its conditioning kwargs; the FM update already matches. (3) Run the O(M*N^2) DP search once (and OSS-ave over a sample batch) to produce a reusable schedule, then plug the resulting timesteps into the existing Euler sampler. Main engineering work is wiring sequence-conditioning into search_OSS and deciding whether to search on the ODE or SDE update; no model training, no new losses.

verifiedPapers.title: Proteina: Scaling Flow-based Protein Structure Generative Models (own reported sampling settings)
verifiedPapers.authors: Geffner, Didi, Zhang, Reidenbach, Cao, Yim, Geiger, Dallago, Kucukbenli, Vahdat, Kreis et al. (NVIDIA)
verifiedPapers.core_idea: This is OUR base model. Reported sampling recipe: linear/OT flow-matching ODE simulated with a first-order Euler integrator; default deployment uses the STOCHASTIC SDE sampler (sampling_mode='sc', score-based with Langevin term) at ~400 integration steps (NFE=400), with the deterministic ODE ('vf') as an alternative. Key tunable knobs exposed: sc_scale_noise (noise/temperature, default 0.35-0.45; trades designability vs diversity), schedule_mode=log, schedule_p=2.0, and the non-uniform training/inference t-distribution p(t)=0.02*U(0,1)+0.98*Beta(1.9,1.0). The gamma noise-injection scale controls SDE-vs-ODE marginal matching (gamma=1 matches ODE distribution). Appendix L ablates t-sampling distributions and g(t) stochasticity schedules. Establishes the baseline NFE we must cut.
verifiedPapers.category: Base model / sampler baseline (the target to accelerate)
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes - this IS the CA-only flow-matching model we are accelerating.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Default ~400 NFE (SDE/Euler). Naively dropping to ~100 degrades quality.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: None - already in our codebase; just the reference point.
verifiedPapers.notes: Schedule/temperature knobs (schedule_mode, schedule_p, sc_scale_noise, gamma) are the first, training-free lever to try for fewer steps before any distillation.

verdict.verdict_summary: CONFIRMED — this is our base model (Proteina, NVIDIA/Mila/MIT, ICLR 2025, arXiv 2503.00710), and every claimed property checks out against both the paper and the local codebase. It is genuinely FLOW-MATCHING (linear/OT interpolant), not score/diffusion/EDM, though it exposes a score-based SDE sampler whose score is derived ANALYTICALLY from the single velocity-net call (vf_to_score, r3n_fm.py:347-367) — so NFE = nsteps (one net eval/step), not 2x. Paper states "For all our experiments, we use N=400" with an Euler-Maruyama scheme on logarithmically-spaced points (sampling_mode='sc' SDE default; 'vf' ODE alt). Training/inference t-distribution p(t)=0.02*U(0,1)+0.98*Beta(1.9,1.0) is exact (paper lines 400/4493; code mix_up02_beta in model_trainer_base.py:1041-1048 with p1=1.9,p2=1.0). Noise scale gamma/sc_scale_noise=0.45 designability-optimal; gamma=1 matches ODE marginals (paper line 4140); App I.4/L.2 ablate g(t) and step size. schedule_mode=log, schedule_p=2.0 confirmed (inference_base.yaml:34-35). ONE NUANCE on the NFE claim: the paper's universal N=400 matches inference_base.yaml (dt=0.0025 -> ceil(1/dt)=400), but the SEQUENCE-CONDITIONED configs this project actually runs default to dt=0.005 -> NFE=200 (e.g. inference_seq_cond_sampling_21-seq.yaml:16). So the realized baseline-to-cut is 200 NFE for our seq-conditioned use, 400 in the paper's reported experiments — both are legitimately 'the default' depending on config lineage. This work establishes the baseline; it is not itself an NFE-reduction method.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: N/A in the usual sense — this IS the flow-matching model, not an external technique imported onto it. It is native OT/linear flow matching (not score/diffusion/EDM). Important subtlety: the default deployed sampler is the stochastic SDE ('sc'/score-based + Langevin term), but the score is obtained analytically from the SAME velocity network via the OT-FM relation v=(1/t)(x_t+scale_ref^2*(1-t)*s) (vf_to_score), so it stays a single-network-eval-per-step flow-matching sampler. The deterministic ODE ('vf') is the alternative. gamma=1 recovers the ODE marginals.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Yes — directly. This is the CA-only protein backbone generator we use; no adaptation needed. Code path r3n_fm.py operates on [*, n, 3] CA coordinates; scale_ref centers the Gaussian reference for CA scale.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Baseline NFE to cut = 400 (paper) / 200 (our seq-conditioned configs, dt=0.005). The paper itself reports NO training-free NFE reduction; N=400 is fixed across all its experiments. Claim that naively dropping to ~100 degrades quality is consistent with the project context and with the follow-up distillation work (arXiv 2510.03095, 'Distilled Protein Backbone Generation') which needs distillation/training to reach few-step generation at >20x speedup — i.e. aggressive step cuts on Proteina are NOT free. Credible training-free headroom from this paper alone is modest (the exposed knobs — schedule_mode=log/schedule_p, g(t), gamma — improve the quality/diversity trade-off at fixed N but were not shown to slash N).
verdict.quality_cost: At the paper's chosen N=400 with gamma=0.45 (or 0.35 for long proteins), quality is the published SOTA designability/diversity. Reducing noise scale (gamma<1) raises designability at the cost of diversity (standard reduced-temperature trade-off, paper App L). Naive step reduction toward ~100 without re-tuning schedule/noise degrades fold/designability quality — the exact regime the downstream template-search pipeline cannot tolerate; this is the problem statement, not a solution.
verdict.implementation_cost: Zero — already implemented and in use. Knobs are live in configs (dt, sampling_mode, sc_scale_noise, schedule_mode, schedule_p, gt_mode/gt_p/gt_clamp_val) and in proteinfoundation/flow_matching/r3n_fm.py. Public code: github.com/NVIDIA-Digital-Bio/proteina (also mirrored at NVIDIA-BioNeMo/proteina).

verifiedPapers.title: Scaling Rectified Flow Transformers for High-Resolution Image Synthesis (Stable Diffusion 3)
verifiedPapers.authors: Patrick Esser, Sumith Kulal, Andreas Blattmann, et al. (Stability AI)
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Two distinct contributions. (1) TRAINING: compares non-uniform timestep-sampling densities for rectified-flow training - uniform, logit-normal(m,s), mode, cosmap - and finds logit-normal centered near t=0 (lognorm(0.00,1.00)) best (emphasizes mid-trajectory). (2) SAMPLING (training-free): a time-SHIFT reparameterization t' = a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) with shift a=sqrt(m/n) scaled by resolution (a=3 at 1024px); larger a pushes steps toward the high-noise end. The shift is the canonical FM analog of EDM's rho.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Highly - this is a NATIVE flow-matching (rectified-flow) formulation, not score/diffusion, so it transfers far more cleanly to our linear-OT FM ODE/SDE than diffusion-sigma works. The sampling-time shift is a one-line training-free knob directly usable on Proteina's Euler integrator. The shift magnitude is a function of effective dimensionality (resolution for images -> roughly sequence length / token count for CA backbones), so it should be tuned per protein length.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Shift enables high-quality few-step sampling at higher resolutions; paper's focus is quality-at-fixed-budget rather than a single headline NFE number. The logit-normal training schedule outperforms uniform-RF and 61 ablated diffusion/FM formulations.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Trivial for the sampling shift (1 line on the time grid; equivalent to a specific schedule_p/loglinear setting). The logit-normal training change only matters if we ever retrain/distill.
verifiedPapers.notes: Most directly on-paradigm reference (rectified flow). The t'=a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) shift is the precise FM time-schedule equivalent of EDM-rho and is exactly the kind of knob to sweep on Proteina. Note the resolution->shift scaling means the optimal shift likely depends on backbone length.

verdict.verdict_summary:
REAL and well-supported. This is the Stable Diffusion 3 paper (Esser et al., "Scaling Rectified Flow Transformers for High-Resolution Image Synthesis," arXiv:2403.03206). Both claimed contributions check out: (1) TRAINING — they ablate 61 formulations and find rectified flow with logit-normal timestep sampling, rf/lognorm(0.00,1.00), consistently top-ranked, beating uniform-RF and LDM-linear/EDM variants. (2) SAMPLING — a training-free time-shift reparameterization t' = a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) with a=sqrt(m/n) (resolution-dependent), and a=3.0 used at 1024px.

TWO claim-wording corrections: (a) lognorm(0.00,1.00) has logit-space mean m=0, which centers the density at t=0.5 (MID-trajectory) — the claim's parenthetical 'emphasizes mid-trajectory' is right, but 'centered near t=0' is a misstatement. (b) The paper applied a=3 during BOTH training and sampling for its flagship model, but the shift is a pure scheduler reparameterization — HF diffusers exposes it as FlowMatchEulerDiscreteScheduler(shift=...), an inference-time knob requiring no retraining. So requires_training=false is correct FOR THE SHIFT; the logit-normal density is a training choice (you can't apply it post-hoc).

It is genuinely flow-matching native (rectified/linear-OT FM), not score/diffusion/EDM — the shift is explicitly the FM analog of EDM's rho/noise-schedule reparameterization, so it transfers cleanly to Proteina's FM ODE/SDE without any score-model assumption. Caveats for our use: NFE/speedup is image-domain only and quality-at-fixed-budget framed (no headline few-step number); the a=sqrt(m/n) resolution scaling is pixel-count-specific and does NOT carry a meaningful protein analog (a becomes a free hyperparameter to tune, not derived). The SDE/stochastic ('sc') sampler Proteina defaults to is not what SD3's shift study targets (SD3 reports an ODE/Euler sampler) — interaction with g(t) noise injection is untested.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: YES — cleanly, and this is its main strength. SD3 is itself a rectified/linear-OT flow-matching model, not score/diffusion/EDM. The time-shift t'=a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) is explicitly presented as the flow-matching analog of EDM's noise-schedule/rho reparameterization, and operates purely on the integration-time grid of the FM ODE — no score function, no EDM preconditioning, no diffusion SNR assumption required. Proteina already exposes schedule_mode/schedule_p knobs that are the same family of monotone time-reparameterizations; the SD3 shift is essentially one more such schedule (it maps onto a power/exponent-style warp). The logit-normal TRAINING density is also FM-native and would apply directly to Proteina's FM training loss. Caveat: SD3's shift was validated with an ODE/Euler sampler; Proteina's default stochastic 'sc' (SDE) sampler with g(t) noise injection is a different setting, so the shift's interaction with the noise schedule is unverified.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: PARTIALLY. The mechanism (training-free time-shift on the FM sampling grid) is domain-agnostic and applies directly to CA-only protein backbone FM sampling — it is just a monotone reparameterization of integration time, identical in spirit to Proteina's existing schedule_mode/schedule_p. What does NOT transfer is the resolution-derivation a=sqrt(m/n): that constant is tied to image pixel count and the signal-destruction argument for images. For proteins there is no pixel-count analog, so the shift strength a becomes a free hyperparameter to sweep rather than a principled value (do NOT port a=3.0 as if it were derived for proteins — it is not). The logit-normal training density similarly transfers as a training-time choice but would require a Proteina retrain/finetune to adopt.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: No headline NFE number is credible to quote for our setting. The SD3 paper frames its results as quality-at-fixed-budget (e.g., the shift and logit-normal schedule improve sample quality/preference at a given step count, and the logit-normal schedule helps more at FEWER steps), NOT as a specific X-step few-step sampler with a measured speedup. The human-preference study only establishes that shift>1.5 is preferred over no-shift at 1024px — not a quantified NFE reduction. For Proteina, the credible expectation is: a well-tuned time-shift may let you reach the same fold quality at somewhat fewer Euler steps than uniform spacing (a schedule-tuning gain, on the order of what schedule_mode/schedule_p already buy), but any claim of e.g. 200->100 NFE at no quality cost is unsubstantiated and must be measured on our CA pipeline. Treat as a free, low-cost schedule knob to sweep, not a guaranteed multiplier.
verdict.quality_cost: For the training-free shift: zero added training cost and, in-domain, it IMPROVES quality at fixed budget (so applied at the trained budget there is no quality penalty; pushed to aggressive few-step it trades off like any coarser schedule). For proteins this is unverified — the risk is that mis-tuned a degrades fold/topology quality the template-search pipeline depends on, so it must be validated, not assumed quality-neutral. The logit-normal TRAINING change is a separate matter: it could improve few-step quality but only after a retrain/finetune, with the usual retraining cost/risk.
verdict.implementation_cost: LOW for the shift (the relevant contribution for our training-free goal): it is ~a few lines reparameterizing the integration-time grid t -> a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) before the Euler steps, conceptually identical to adding another schedule_mode option in Proteina's sampler. Sweep a over a small grid (e.g. 1.5-6) at reduced NFE and measure fold quality. Public reference code exists: Stability-AI/sd3-ref and the HF diffusers SD3 pipeline (FlowMatchEulerDiscreteScheduler exposes shift= as an inference arg) — directly readable for the exact mapping. MEDIUM-HIGH cost for the logit-normal training density, since it requires modifying Proteina's FM training timestep sampling and retraining/finetuning. Open question to validate before relying on it: how the shift composes with Proteina's default stochastic 'sc'/SDE sampler and g(t) noise schedule, which SD3 did not study.

verifiedPapers.title: Flows, straight but not so fast: Exploring the Design Space of Rectified Flows in Protein Design
verifiedPapers.authors: Junhua Chen, Simon Mathis, Charles Harris, Kieran Didi, Pietro Lio
verifiedPapers.category: flow-matching inference schedule + ReFlow for protein backbones
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Systematic study of the low-NFE design space for SE(3) flow-matching protein backbone models (FoldFlow-OT, QFlow). Two separable findings: (A) TRAINING-FREE - a 'Noise-Focused' time discretization (finer steps near t=0, the noise end, where SO(3) trajectory curvature is highest) beats uniform/data-focused/edge-focused for both models; inference annealing (velocity scaling, lowering effective temperature) trades designability vs diversity. (B) ReFlow rectification TRAINING is needed for the largest low-NFE gains and is sensitive to coupling-generation direction and annealing; image-domain ReFlow tricks do NOT transfer to proteins.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Directly - same domain (SE(3)/frame flow-matching protein backbones). The Noise-Focused discretization and annealing findings are training-free and immediately testable on Proteina. Caveat: their models are frame-based (Ca,C,N,O), whereas Proteina is CA-only, and 'noise-focused' (steps near t=0) is the OPPOSITE end from EDM/AYS 'low-noise/near-data' concentration because of the FM time convention - verify the time direction carefully before porting.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: FoldFlow-OT @15 NFE: designability 0.584->0.664 (rectified); QFlow @20 NFE: 0.528->0.780, scRMSD 1.74A. High-NFE (50-100) performance DEGRADES after rectification - rectification is a low-NFE-only win.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Low for the training-free discretization/annealing sweep (closest direct precedent for what we want); high if we pursue ReFlow rectification (needs retraining + careful coupling/annealing).
verifiedPapers.notes: The single most on-target paper: same modality, same goal (cut NFE without losing designability), and it isolates which schedule choices are training-free vs need retraining. Strongly recommend reproducing their Noise-Focused-vs-uniform discretization + annealing sweep on Proteina first. Watch the t-direction convention mismatch.

verdict.verdict_summary:
REAL and accurately described. Paper = arXiv 2510.24732 "Flows, straight but not so fast: Exploring the Design Space of Rectified Flows in Protein Design" (Chen, Mathis, Harris, Didi, Lio; Cambridge), an extended version of the ICML 2025 GenBio Workshop paper "Straight but not so fast: Challenges with Rectified Flows in Protein Design" (OpenReview nvl3Bt6EBG). It studies the low-NFE design space of two SE(3)^N flow-matching protein BACKBONE-FRAME models, FoldFlow-OT and QFlow/ReQFlow.

The two separable findings are confirmed. (A) TRAINING-FREE: a 'Noise-Focused' time discretization (finer steps near t=0, where Euclidean trajectory curvature + annealed SO(3) velocity are highest) beats uniform/data-focused/edge-focused for both models, and inference annealing (rotational velocity scaling) trades designability up vs diversity down. These are pure inference-time knobs on a pretrained model. (B) ReFlow rectification (generate noise-data couplings from the base model, then FINE-TUNE on them) is genuinely needed for the largest low-NFE gains, is sensitive to coupling-generation direction (inverted/reverse coupling that works for images collapses FoldFlow designability to 0.000 because reversed integration yields non-Gaussian latents) and to annealing, and image-domain ReFlow tricks do NOT transfer. Confirmed.

NUMBERS mostly check out, with one framing nuance: the claimed 'FoldFlow-OT @15 NFE 0.584->0.664' is real but 0.584 is itself the Noise-Focused (training-free) score at 15 NFE — the naive uniform baseline at 15 NFE is ~0.076 — and the 0.664 uses annealing=3. QFlow @20 NFE 0.528->0.780 designability with scRMSD ~3.72A->1.74A is confirmed. High-NFE (50-100) DEGRADATION after rectification is confirmed (FoldFlow 50 NFE 0.816->0.776), so rectification is explicitly a low-NFE-only win — a real and important caveat. Claimed 'requires_training: false' is only HALF true: the headline numbers (rectification) require training; the discretization/annealing part is training-free.

RELEVANCE TO PROTEINA (the asker's use case): LOW. These are SE(3) FRAME models (rotation+translation per residue, 4-atom backbone), NOT Proteina's CA-only linear/OT Euclidean flow. The training-free Noise-Focused-discretization INSIGHT (cluster steps where velocity/curvature is largest) is conceptually portable and worth trying on Proteina's Euler scheduler at zero cost, but the specific 'noise end t=0' optimum is justified here by SO(3) rotational curvature + rotation-annealing dynamics that Proteina's pure-Euclidean CA flow does not have, so the location of the optimum will NOT transfer unchanged and must be re-derived empirically. The big rectification gains require a full ReFlow fine-tune (synthetic coupling generation + retraining) — out of scope for a training-free speedup goal. No public code found.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: Directly applies to flow-matching (not score/diffusion/EDM): both models are SE(3)^N optimal-transport/linear flow-matching samplers integrated with an Euler ODE/SDE scheme, the same family as Proteina. The paper is explicitly framed around flow matching and ReFlow, not diffusion. So the conceptual machinery (time discretization, velocity-scaling annealing, ReFlow rectification) is native to the flow-matching ODE/SDE setting and does not assume a score/EDM formulation.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: PARTIAL / indirect. The models studied operate on SE(3) backbone FRAMES (per-residue rotation + translation, 4 backbone atoms N/CA/C/O), NOT on CA-only coordinates like Proteina. The translational sub-flow is Euclidean and analogous to Proteina's CA flow, but the core 'Noise-Focused near t=0' justification rests on SO(3) rotational curvature and rotation-velocity annealing that a CA-only Euclidean model lacks. So findings do not map one-to-one; the general principle (concentrate Euler steps where the velocity field is stiffest) is transferable, but where that region sits for CA-only must be measured, not assumed.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: For the FRAME models studied: training-free Noise-Focused discretization + annealing makes 15-20 NFE usable (FoldFlow-OT 0.584 designability @15 NFE vs 0.076 uniform; QFlow 0.528 @20). ReFlow rectification (needs training) pushes QFlow @20 NFE to 0.780 designability / ~1.74A scRMSD and FoldFlow-OT @15 to ~0.664. Net credible low-NFE operating point ~15-20 NFE (vs typical 100-500), i.e. roughly a 5-10x step reduction for these frame models. Sibling work ReQFlow (arXiv 2502.14637) reports up to ~37-62x wall-clock speedup vs RFDiffusion/Genie2 at length 300 via the same rectify-then-low-NFE idea. For Proteina specifically, NO number is credible without re-tuning: only the zero-cost discretization-reshaping idea is portable, and any rectification speedup would require a Proteina-side ReFlow fine-tune.
verdict.quality_cost: Low-NFE rectification trades away high-NFE quality: rectified models DEGRADE at 50-100 NFE (FoldFlow-OT 50 NFE 0.816->0.776), so it is strictly a low-NFE regime tool. Annealing raises designability but LOWERS diversity (FoldFlow annealing=10: designability 0.816 but diversity drops), a designability-vs-diversity trade the asker's template-search/fold-topology pipeline must weigh — over-annealing can collapse diversity and bias toward a narrow set of easy folds. The training-free discretization change alone has no quality downside (pure step reallocation at fixed NFE) and is the safest lever.
verdict.implementation_cost: Training-free part (Noise-Focused discretization + velocity/annealing scaling): LOW for Proteina — both are inference-time scheduler edits. Proteina already exposes schedule_mode/schedule_p and a g(t) SDE noise schedule, so reshaping the step distribution toward the high-curvature region and adding a velocity-scaling knob is a small change to the Euler sampler with no retraining. BUT the optimal step concentration must be re-derived empirically for CA-only Euclidean flow (their 't=0 noise end' optimum is frame/SO(3)-specific). Rectification part: HIGH — requires generating a synthetic noise-data coupling dataset with the base Proteina model and a full ReFlow fine-tune, plus care on coupling direction (inverted coupling can collapse to non-Gaussian latents and zero designability) and annealing; out of scope for a purely training-free speedup.

verifiedPapers.title: On the Trajectory Regularity of ODE-based Diffusion Sampling (GITS: Geometry-Inspired Time Scheduling)
verifiedPapers.authors: Defang Chen, Zhenyu Zhou, Can Wang, Chunhua Shen, Siwei Lyu
verifiedPapers.category: Geometry/curvature-based step placement via dynamic programming
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Observes that ODE sampling trajectories have strong geometric regularity (a 'boomerang' shape). GITS allocates larger steps where trajectory curvature is small and smaller steps where curvature is large, formulating discretization as a shortest-path problem that minimizes accumulated local truncation error, solved by dynamic programming over a candidate time grid (one hyperparameter 'coeff'). Minimal modification to any ODE solver, negligible compute, strong gains at 5-10 NFE.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising. GITS is solver-agnostic and only needs the model's own trajectory geometry, which exists equally for a flow-matching ODE. The DP over a candidate grid to minimize accumulated local truncation error transfers cleanly; we would build the cost matrix by running a handful of reference trajectories from our Proteina CA model and applying the same DP. No image-specific assumptions in the core algorithm. Main caveat: cost matrix uses a fixed reference (deviates from true global-error optimum), and the 'boomerang' regularity should be verified for CA-coordinate trajectories.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Targets 5-10 NFE; reported superior FID vs uniform/heuristic schedules in the few-step regime at negligible added cost.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Low. Build a cost matrix from reference trajectories + run a small DP; integrates into existing Euler/Heun samplers with little code.
verifiedPapers.public_code_url: https://github.com/zju-pi/diff-sampler (gits-main); also a GITS scheduler PR in ComfyUI
verifiedPapers.notes: Highly transferable and training-free, with public code. The DP-over-trajectory-geometry idea is one of the cleanest few-step schedule-search methods and does not assume score/EDM in its core, making it a strong direct candidate for our flow-matching CA model. Note GITS minimizes accumulated LOCAL error (not strictly global) — OSS below addresses that.

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and accurately described. "On the Trajectory Regularity of ODE-based Diffusion Sampling" (Chen et al., ICML 2024; arXiv 2405.11326), code at github.com/zju-pi/diff-sampler/gits-main + ComfyUI PR #3769 (GITSScheduler). GITS exploits the low-dimensional "boomerang" regularity of ODE sampling trajectories and reallocates timestamps via a minimum-cost-path/dynamic-programming search over a fine candidate time grid (one hyperparameter coeff, default ~1.15-1.20; larger = more biased toward t=0), minimizing accumulated local truncation error. It is a training-free, solver-agnostic time-SCHEDULE (drop-in for Euler/Heun/iPNDM/DPM-Solver(++)/DEIS/UniPC). Claim of "negligible compute" is mostly true but NOT zero: it needs a ONE-TIME warmup (default num_warmup=256 samples via a teacher solver, default 61-step iPNDM) to estimate the trajectory and build the DP cost matrix — one-time/amortized, not per-sample. Strong few-step gains are credible (CIFAR-10 iPNDM 13.59->8.38 FID at NFE=5; 2.77->2.49 at NFE=10; DDIM 49.66->28.05 at NFE=5), largest at 5-8 NFE and shrinking by NFE~10+. THE CRITICAL CAVEAT FOR PROTEINA: GITS is built on and coded against the EDM/Karras score-diffusion framework (sigma/denoiser parameterization); all experiments are images only (CIFAR-10/FFHQ/ImageNet); no flow-matching, rectified-flow, protein, molecule, audio, or 3D validation exists, and the ComfyUI author explicitly did NOT test rectified-flow/FM models.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: PARTIAL / not drop-in. The underlying phenomenon GITS exploits — strong geometric "boomerang" regularity of the sampling ODE trajectory — is a property of integrating a smooth velocity field and plausibly carries over to Proteina's linear-OT flow-matching ODE (and a 2025 follow-up, arXiv 2506.10177, generalizes the geometric-regularity observation). However GITS's *implementation* is EDM-specific: its local-truncation-error cost function, the sigma/noise candidate grid, and the teacher reference solver are all written in the diffusion/EDM parameterization. Porting to FM requires reimplementing the DP cost over Proteina's FM time-t (and g(t) for the SDE) parameterization and re-running the warmup; it is a genuine reimplementation, not a config flag. No published evidence it has been applied to flow matching. Note Proteina's default sampler is STOCHASTIC (SDE/"sc"); GITS is designed for the deterministic PF-ODE, so the schedule's optimality argument (boomerang determinism) is cleanest for the ODE/deterministic mode — using it with the SDE adds an untested assumption.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Plausibly yes in principle — GITS is data-modality-agnostic at the math level (it operates on the trajectory geometry in the latent/coordinate space, not on image-specific structure), so CA-only backbone coordinates are not an obstacle. But it has NEVER been tested on proteins/molecules/3D; all reported results are images. The one-time warmup needs a quality metric to validate the chosen schedule — for Proteina you'd validate against the downstream fold/topology metric (e.g., template-search hit rate / designability / scTM), not FID, which changes the warmup tuning loop.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Credible target is the 5-10 NFE regime, matching the paper. Realistic expectation if ported to Proteina: meaningful quality recovery when cutting from ~200 NFE toward the low-step regime relative to a naive uniform/log schedule, with the largest relative gains at the lowest NFE (5-8) and diminishing returns by ~10-15 NFE. The published image speedups (e.g., reaching iPNDM's ~10-NFE quality at fewer steps) do NOT directly numerically transfer to a flow-matching protein model — only the qualitative "better schedule beats uniform at low NFE" claim is supported. It is a schedule optimizer, not a distillation method, so it will not by itself reach the 1-4 NFE extreme regime.
verdict.quality_cost: Low-to-moderate, regime-dependent. In its tested domain GITS IMPROVES quality at fixed low NFE (it is a Pareto-improving schedule, not a quality-for-speed trade), so at matched NFE it should not degrade and may help. The real costs/risks for Proteina: (1) the schedule is tuned to a teacher trajectory + a quality metric on a warmup set, so it can overfit the warmup distribution and the chosen coeff; (2) optimality is argued for the deterministic ODE, so coupling it with Proteina's default stochastic SDE sampler is unvalidated and could interact with the g(t) noise schedule; (3) gains shrink as NFE rises, so above ~15-20 NFE the benefit over a good log/edm schedule may be marginal. Net: unlikely to hurt topology quality at matched NFE if validated on the downstream fold metric, but must be re-tuned (coeff + warmup) on Proteina rather than copied.
verdict.implementation_cost: MODERATE. Not a config flag for Proteina. Required work: (1) reimplement the DP cost function (local truncation error: Euler step vs. teacher prediction) in Proteina's flow-matching time/noise parameterization; (2) define the candidate time grid and a teacher solver (a high-NFE Proteina run); (3) run the one-time warmup (~tens-to-256 samples) to build the cost matrix and solve the DP for the target NFE; (4) wire the resulting custom timestamp schedule into Proteina's existing schedule_mode knob (it already accepts custom schedules, which lowers the integration cost); (5) decide ODE-vs-SDE handling and re-tune coeff against the downstream template-search/designability metric. Reference code (gits-main) exists and is clean but is EDM-coded, so it serves as an algorithmic guide rather than reusable-as-is. Estimate: a few days of focused engineering plus warmup compute.

verifiedPapers.title: Learning to Discretize Denoising Diffusion ODEs (LD3)
verifiedPapers.authors: Vinh Tong, Trung-Dung Hoang, Anji Liu, Guy Van den Broeck, Mathias Niepert
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Treats the discretization time points as trainable parameters and optimizes them by backpropagating through a differentiable ODE-solving pipeline to directly minimize global truncation error (teacher high-NFE vs student few-NFE). Introduces a surrogate loss L_soft to fix underfitting from direct global-error optimization, proven close to the true distillation objective. Does NOT retrain the diffusion model.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising and arguably the cleanest fit conceptually. The only thing 'trained' is a small set of timesteps (a handful of scalars) via differentiable ODE solving; the base velocity network is frozen. Since our Proteina sampler is already an Euler-integrated ODE/SDE, making it differentiable w.r.t. the time grid is mechanically straightforward, and the global-truncation-error objective is domain-agnostic (works on CA coordinates as the state). Very cheap (5-10 min training reported on images). Main work: implement the differentiable solver pass and L_soft surrogate for the FM ODE.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Matches/beats baselines at 10 NFE (FID 2.38 CIFAR10, 2.27 AFHQv2 at 10 NFE); learns the schedule in only 5-10 minutes of training on a single GPU.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Moderate. Needs a differentiable ODE-solver pass + surrogate loss, but optimizes only ~N timestep scalars; very cheap wall-clock.
verifiedPapers.notes: Best 'learning-to-discretize' entry: directly minimizes global truncation error, frozen base model, tiny trainable parameter count, minutes of training, solver-agnostic. Strong direct candidate — the FM ODE just becomes the differentiable pipeline. Listed as requires_training=true only because the few timestep scalars are learned (not the model).

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and well-credentialed. LD3 ("Learning to Discretize Denoising Diffusion ODEs," Tong et al., arXiv 2405.15506) is an ICLR 2025 ORAL with public MIT-licensed code (github.com/vinhsuhi/LD3). All claimed properties verify: timesteps treated as trainable parameters, optimized by backpropagating through a differentiable ODE-solving pipeline to minimize global truncation error (high-NFE teacher vs few-NFE student); the L_soft surrogate fixes underfitting from direct global-error optimization and is proven close to the true distillation objective; the pretrained model is NOT retrained. The headline numbers match exactly (FID 2.38 @10 NFE CIFAR10, 2.27 @10 NFE AFHQv2; schedule learned in ~5-10 min on a single GPU). It is solver-agnostic (Euler, DPM-Solver++, UniPC, IPNDM) and the paper additionally reports 3D point-cloud generation and molecular docking, not images only. The one nuance vs the claim: "requires_training" is TRUE but means lightweight per-model SCHEDULE optimization (frozen weights, ~100-200 samples), not distillation/retraining of the network.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Credible. LD3 demonstrably reaches strong quality at 10 NFE and is benchmarked down to NFE=4-7 (e.g., CIFAR10 NFE=6 FID improves 13.46->7.51 with L_soft + decoupled steps). Against Proteina's NFE~200 default this is a nominal 20x reduction, but that headline is from image diffusion and is NOT directly portable. A defensible, credible target for Proteina would be a learned schedule that holds fold/topology quality at ~50-100 NFE (2-4x speedup), with single-digit NFE being aspirational and unproven for CA backbones. The 5-10 min/1-GPU schedule-training cost is real and small; the recurring inference speedup is the payoff. The teacher (high-NFE Proteina) must be run once to generate reference CA trajectories for the optimization.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: Partial / plausible-but-unproven. LD3 is formulated for the score/diffusion probability-flow ODE (EDM/Karras parameterization) and optimizes the time discretization (plus a decoupled per-step coupling and a perturbed initial state x'_T) by backprop through the deterministic ODE solver. The optimized object is the integration schedule, which is conceptually solver- and parameterization-portable: a flow-matching linear/OT ODE integrated with Euler is the same kind of deterministic ODE, and Proteina already exposes schedule_mode/schedule_p knobs that LD3 would replace with a learned discretization. BUT: (a) zero flow-matching/rectified-flow experiments exist in the paper or repo, so transfer is untested; (b) LD3's truncation-error objective is defined on the deterministic ODE, so Proteina's DEFAULT stochastic 'sc'/SDE sampler with noise injection breaks the assumption — you would need to run LD3 against the deterministic flow-matching ODE (g(t)=0) or restrict optimization to the ODE-equivalent path. Net: the schedule-learning idea transfers in principle; the published guarantees and code paths do not cover SDE flow-matching out of the box.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Yes in principle, with caveats. Nothing in LD3 is image-specific: it operates on the ODE trajectory in the model's state space, and the paper already shows 3D point-cloud generation and molecular docking, so CA-only (3xN_residue) coordinate trajectories are well within its modality range. The learned schedule is per-model, so it must be optimized specifically for the Proteina checkpoint and the relevant length regime. Main risk is that the global-truncation-error / FID-style objective optimizes for distributional fidelity, which may not perfectly align with the downstream template-search fold/topology metric the user cares about — the LD3 loss would need to be evaluated against (or the validation set chosen to reflect) that fold-quality criterion.
verdict.implementation_cost: Moderate. Steps: (1) expose Proteina's flow-matching ODE as a differentiable Euler-integration pipeline (deterministic path, g(t)=0) so timesteps are leaf tensors with gradients — Proteina's sampler is already Euler-based, so this is mostly wiring autograd through the integration loop and the CA velocity field; (2) generate ~100-200 high-NFE teacher CA trajectories as reference targets; (3) port LD3's L_soft + decoupled-timestep optimization (its solver wrappers are written for EDM/score ODEs and DPM-Solver/UniPC, so the velocity-field call and the linear/OT flow-matching parameterization must be substituted); (4) pick a validation metric aligned with fold/topology (not just distributional FID). Estimated a few engineer-days to a week. Biggest unknowns: adapting LD3's solver/parameterization code to flow matching and handling the SDE-default sampler (must switch to the deterministic ODE for the learned schedule, then verify the deterministic-ODE samples still meet the downstream pipeline's quality bar).
verdict.quality_cost: Low-to-moderate and tunable. By construction LD3 minimizes global truncation error vs the high-NFE teacher, so a learned schedule is designed to PRESERVE quality at the chosen NFE rather than degrade it — in images it matches/beats baselines at 10 NFE. The risks for Proteina: (a) aggressive NFE cuts (single digits) on CA backbones are unvalidated and may cost fold accuracy; (b) the objective targets distributional similarity, which may not be a perfect proxy for template-search fold/topology quality; (c) abandoning the default stochastic SDE sampler for the deterministic ODE could itself shift sample quality and diversity, independent of NFE. Mitigation: validate the learned schedule directly on the downstream template-search metric and choose the NFE where it holds (likely ~50-100, i.e. a safe 2-4x, before chasing larger cuts).

verifiedPapers.title: Differentiable Solver Search for Fast Diffusion Sampling
verifiedPapers.authors: Shuai Wang, Zexian Li, Qipeng Zhang, Tianhui Song, Xubin Li, Tiezheng Ge, Bo Zheng, Limin Wang
verifiedPapers.category: Co-optimization of time-step schedule AND solver coefficients (differentiable search)
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Shows standard t-related Lagrange interpolation is suboptimal and identifies a compact search space of {time steps, solver coefficients}. Uses a differentiable search to jointly find the optimal schedule and solver coefficients on a frozen pretrained model, without harming pretraining. The searched solver+schedule transfers across architectures, resolutions, and model sizes.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Strong fit. Explicitly validated on rectified-flow / flow-matching backbones (SiT-XL/2, FlowDCN-XL/2) in addition to DDPM/DiT — directly relevant since Proteina is flow-matching. Only a few solver coefficients + timesteps are searched (base model frozen), and results transfer across model sizes/resolutions, suggesting a search done on a calibration set could yield a reusable few-step schedule+solver for our CA model. Caveat: validated on image/latent generation; CA-coordinate state and SDE (stochastic) sampling would need testing; method is most natural for the ODE/deterministic case.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 10-step FID 2.40 (SiT-XL/2, rectified flow) and 2.35 (FlowDCN-XL/2) on ImageNet-256, 2.33 (DiT-XL/2) — i.e. high quality at NFE=10.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Moderate. Differentiable search over a small parameter set on a calibration set; base model frozen. More involved than a pure schedule search since it also tunes solver coefficients.
verifiedPapers.public_code_url: Referenced in paper / OpenReview (https://openreview.net/forum?id=FB84Wkn3Xp); repo from Nanjing University + Alibaba
verifiedPapers.notes: Among the most directly applicable: explicitly works on flow-matching (rectified-flow) models and co-optimizes WHERE the steps go plus the solver, hitting NFE=10 at high quality. If we are willing to also tune solver coefficients (not just timesteps) this is arguably the best-matched method for our FM CA setting. Listed requires_training=true (small coefficient/timestep search, model frozen).

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL work, accurately described, but with two caveats. This is "Differentiable Solver Search for Fast Diffusion Sampling" (arXiv 2505.21114, ICML 2025, MCG-NJU + Nanjing Univ + Alibaba; code at github.com/MCG-NJU/NeuralSolver, MIT license, searched coefficients released). The claimed OpenReview ID in the prompt (FB84Wkn3Xp) is wrong — the real one is oUApObDC1e — but the paper, claims, and NFE=10 FID numbers (SiT-XL/2 2.40, FlowDCN-XL/2 2.35, DiT-XL/2 2.33 on ImageNet-256) all check out. Core idea is as described: it shows Adams-like t-Lagrange interpolation is suboptimal, reduces the solver to a compact search space of {timesteps t_i, lower-triangular coefficient matrix c_ij} (~N^2/2 free params, e.g. ~55 for N=10), and uses differentiable search to minimize MSE against a Euler-250 reference ODE trajectory on a FROZEN pretrained model. Transfer across architectures/resolutions/sizes is demonstrated (search on FlowDCN-B/2 transfers to SiT-XL/2; 256->512). CAVEAT 1 (terminology): "requires_training: true" is misleading — the generative model is never touched/retrained; only ~50 scalar solver params are optimized (~30 min on 8xH20, needing only a teacher Euler-250 trajectory, no ground-truth data). It is training-FREE for the model, a lightweight offline calibration. CAVEAT 2 (quality framing): the NFE=10 numbers are good but not uniformly state-of-the-art — for SiT-XL/2 the plain Adam4 baseline already gets 2.27 at NFE=10, BETTER than the searched solver's 2.40; the genuine win is on DiT (2.33 vs UniPC ~2.74). Converged SiT-XL/2 FID is ~2.06, so a real residual gap remains at 10 steps. CRITICAL for Proteina: the method is DETERMINISTIC ODE ONLY — no SDE/stochastic-noise-injection variant exists. Proteina's default sampler is a stochastic SDE ('sc') with g(t) noise injection, so the searched solver does NOT drop in; it would only apply to Proteina's deterministic-ODE sampling path (or one would have to forgo noise injection). It is parameterization-flexible (native v-prediction/flow-matching; DDPM via x-param), so it transfers cleanly to Proteina's linear-OT flow-matching ODE in principle.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: YES for the ODE path — the method is built natively for rectified-flow / flow-matching v-prediction (SiT-XL/2, FlowDCN-XL/2 are exactly this), and Proteina's linear optimal-transport flow-matching ODE matches that setting. The searched {timesteps, coefficients} are model-prediction-agnostic enough to extend to score/DDPM (DiT-XL/2) too. BUT it is strictly DETERMINISTIC-ODE: no stochastic/SDE variant is provided. Proteina's default 'sc'/SDE sampler with g(t) noise injection is outside the method's scope, so transfer is clean only if you sample with the deterministic ODE (or accept dropping noise injection). This is the single biggest applicability caveat.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Plausibly yes, but UNTESTED outside images. The solver is data-modality-agnostic in principle — it operates purely on the integrator (timesteps + coefficient matrix) of the probability-flow ODE, independent of whether the state is image pixels or CA coordinates. Nothing in the math is image-specific. However, all experiments are class-conditional/text-to-image image generation; there is NO molecular/protein/3D/point-cloud evaluation, and the search supervision (MSE-to-Euler-250 reference trajectory) was validated against an image-FID quality target, not a fold/topology metric. For CA-only backbone generation you would search against a Proteina Euler-N reference trajectory and must re-validate that low-NFE outputs preserve the fold/topology your template-search pipeline needs (the searched solver optimizes trajectory-MSE, not designability/scTM/template-recall).
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: Credible target: NFE~10 deterministic ODE on a flow-matching model with quality close to the converged result — i.e., a ~20x reduction from a 200-250-step reference toward ~10 steps, or more conservatively a solid 2-3x cut (200->~60-100, and plausibly ->~20-30) at near-lossless trajectory match. The headline image FIDs (2.33-2.40 at NFE=10 vs ~2.06 converged) are real but on images; for Proteina the credible, defensible claim is roughly NFE 200 -> ~20-50 with minimal fold-quality loss, with NFE~10 being aspirational and requiring empirical fold-quality validation. Note the searched solver must be re-run per target NFE.
verdict.quality_cost: Modest but nonzero, and not free of caveats. On images at NFE=10: residual FID gap of ~0.3-0.4 vs converged (~2.06), and for SiT-XL/2 specifically the searched solver (2.40) is actually slightly WORSE than the simple Adam4 baseline (2.27) at the same NFE — the method's clear win is on DiT/DDPM, less so on the rectified-flow models most analogous to Proteina. For protein CA generation the relevant cost is unknown: trajectory-MSE minimization does not guarantee preservation of fold/topology/designability, so quality cost must be measured with the downstream template-search metric, not assumed from FID.
verdict.implementation_cost: Moderate. (1) One-time offline search: generate a Proteina Euler-250 (or Euler-N) reference-trajectory dataset of CA samples, then run the differentiable search (~50 scalar params, lower-triangular coeff matrix + softmaxed timesteps) minimizing MSE to the reference — cheap (their image version is ~30 min on 8 GPUs; protein CA state is far smaller). (2) Integrate the resulting fixed {t_i, c_ij} into Proteina's Euler integration loop as a linear-multistep update — small code change. (3) MUST run on the DETERMINISTIC ODE path; adapting to Proteina's default SDE/noise-injection sampler is NOT supported and would be research-grade work. (4) Re-search per target NFE. (5) Validate low-NFE fold quality against the template-search pipeline before trusting it. No model retraining/distillation required.

verifiedPapers.title: Diffusion is spectral autoregression
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Argues that in the visual domain diffusion sampling is approximate autoregression in the spatial-frequency domain. Because natural-image spectra follow a power law (~f^-alpha) while Gaussian noise is spectrally flat, increasing noise acts as a frequency-dependent low-pass filter: at HIGH noise only low frequencies (coarse/global structure) survive and are generated first; at LOW noise only high frequencies (fine detail) remain to be reconstructed. Implies the noise level directly indexes which spatial scale the model is committing, and that perceptually important coarse structure is decided in the mid-to-high-noise regime.
verifiedPapers.category: Spectral / coarse-to-fine analysis of noise levels
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Partial. The power-law-vs-flat-noise argument is general to any signal with a non-flat power spectrum. CA-only protein backbones are 1D chains of 3D points; their spectral structure (low-frequency = overall fold/radius-of-gyration, high-frequency = local zig-zag) plausibly follows the same logic, but the paper itself is image/audio-only, so transfer is by analogy not demonstrated.
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Low (conceptual framing to motivate spending more integration steps in the coarse/high-noise regime that sets topology)
verifiedPapers.notes: Foundational primary source for THIS angle. Directly supports the hypothesis that global fold/topology is decided at high noise and that step budget should be skewed accordingly. Pairs with Falck's rebuttal below for balance.

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and accurately described. This is Sander Dieleman's blog post "Diffusion is spectral autoregression" (sander.ai, 2 Sep 2024), with an accompanying Colab. The claimed mechanism is exactly right: natural-image spectra follow a power law P(f)∝f^-α (α≈2) while Gaussian noise is spectrally flat, so increasing noise acts as a frequency-dependent low-pass filter — at high noise only low frequencies (coarse/global structure) survive and are generated first; at low noise only high frequencies (fine detail) remain. The work is conceptual and training-free, and makes NO quantitative NFE/speedup claim (the "None (conceptual)" claim is correct). It is a lens/intuition, not a sampler. CAVEATS the author and critics raise: (a) it is "approximate," valid only in expectation averaged over many images, with a smooth (non-sharp) frequency elbow; (b) Fabian Falck's rebuttal ("Diffusion is not necessarily Spectral Autoregression") and the ICML paper "A Fourier Space Perspective on Diffusion Models" (arXiv 2505.11278) show the frequency hierarchy is a DATA-DEPENDENT implicit choice, not a necessity — an EqualSNR schedule that corrupts all frequencies equally matches DDPM quality, and the faster high-freq corruption even mildly violates the reverse-process Gaussianity assumption. Peyman Milanfar disputes the "autoregression" label itself. Bottom line for our goal: it is a legitimate conceptual framing that transfers cleanly to flow matching, but on its own it yields no concrete NFE-reduction recipe and gives no quality guarantees.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: YES — cleanly, and this is its biggest strength relative to EDM-specific tricks. Dieleman explicitly uses rectified-flow / flow matching as a worked example, and the underlying argument is about the interaction between the data power spectrum and the (flat) noise spectrum in the FORWARD corruption process, which is identical for diffusion and linear/OT flow matching. The Fourier-space paper frames the same effect via per-frequency SNR(t), which exists for any FM/diffusion noising. No score/EDM-specific assumption is required for the core lens. (Note: it does NOT inherit FM's specific ODE; it is a property of the forward path, so it applies to our Euler-integrated OT-FM sampler.)
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: CONJECTURAL, not demonstrated. The Fourier-space paper lists proteins among power-law modalities (shows Cryo-EM density maps, which are gridded volumes) but presents ZERO protein generative experiments — motivation-vs-validation gap. More importantly, Proteina operates on CA-only coordinate TRACES, which are an ordered point set / 1D sequence of 3D points, NOT a gridded image, so there is no canonical 2D/3D Fourier basis and no established power-law spectrum for CA backbones. To apply the lens you would first have to define a spectral representation (e.g., Fourier along residue index, graph-Laplacian / normal-mode spectrum of the contact graph, or a coordinate-grid voxelization) and empirically verify a power-law-like decay — none of which is given. The coarse-to-fine intuition is plausible and aligns with existing hierarchical protein methods (DiffTopo, LSD/latent-structure diffusion), but that is independent prior art, not a result of this blog.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: NONE directly from this work — it proposes no sampler and reports no NFE or speedup. It is purely diagnostic. The only actionable, indirect implication for our NFE-cutting goal: because the noise level indexes which spatial scale is being committed, and the perceptually/topologically important COARSE structure is decided in the mid-to-high-noise regime, one could in principle allocate integration steps NON-UNIFORMLY — denser steps in the high-noise (coarse, fold/topology-determining) window and sparser in the low-noise (fine-detail) window. That is exactly what Proteina's existing schedule_mode knobs (log, power/schedule_p, cos_sch_v_snr, edm) already let you do, so the credible payoff is "informed schedule tuning," not a new method. Realistic upside is modest (the kind of single-digit-to-~2x step reductions schedule tuning typically buys), and is NOT quantified or guaranteed by this source. The competing EqualSNR finding even suggests the frequency hierarchy is not load-bearing, which weakens the case that aggressive coarse-vs-fine step reallocation will reliably help.
verdict.quality_cost: UNQUANTIFIED by the source (no benchmarks, no FID/NFE curves — it is conceptual). For our pipeline the relevant risk is concrete: if a schedule informed by this lens under-resolves the high-noise/low-frequency window, it directly damages global fold/topology — precisely the property the downstream template-search depends on. So any step-budget reallocation must be validated empirically (designability / fold-recovery / TM to intended topology), since the blog provides no guarantee and the EqualSNR result cautions that the coarse-first hierarchy may be an implicit, swappable design choice rather than a fixed structure to exploit.
verdict.implementation_cost: LOW-to-MODERATE if used only as INTUITION to guide existing schedule knobs (schedule_mode / schedule_p / g(t)): essentially zero code, just sweep configs — but you must first decide a spectral representation for CA traces to even check whether the premise (power-law spectrum, coarse-first ordering) holds for Proteina, which is a small empirical study (compute per-frequency or per-mode SNR(t) along the FM path on CA coordinates). HIGH cost if you try to build an actual frequency-domain sampler or EqualSNR-style reweighting for CA backbones: that needs a principled spectral basis for irregular 3D point sets and likely retraining, which is out of scope for a training-free speedup. Recommendation: treat as a conceptual prior for non-uniform step allocation favoring the high-noise/coarse regime, then validate fold quality empirically; do not expect a turnkey NFE win.

verifiedPapers.title: Noise schedules considered harmful
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Reframes the 'noise schedule' as just one of several knobs (model parameterization, loss weighting, timestep-sampling distribution, sampling spacing) that jointly decide how much capacity/compute each noise level gets. Key actionable claim: in noise-level ranges that are MORE important (perceptually relevant low frequencies / harder-to-predict levels, and levels where errors compound) you should space sampling steps closer together to evaluate the model more there. Argues uniform spacing is suboptimal and points to logSNR-uniform spacing and analytic step-optimization (Align Your Steps) as better. Also notes optimal training-time vs sampling-time emphasis differ.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes, in principle. The argument is solver- and modality-agnostic: identify which noise levels are most error-prone / most decisive for the downstream metric (here fold topology) and concentrate steps there. Directly motivates a non-uniform Euler step schedule for our flow ODE/SDE.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None directly; cites Align Your Steps / EDM as the concrete mechanisms
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Low conceptual; the operational version is just choosing a non-uniform schedule_mode/schedule_p (knobs we already expose).
verifiedPapers.notes: Most directly actionable conceptual source for our project: it tells us the right lever is WHERE to place the limited Euler steps, not the count alone, and that the placement should follow noise-level importance (which for us = fold-setting mid/high noise).

verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: YES — cleanly.
