The post is explicitly framed in domain-agnostic logSNR (λ) terms and unifies VP / VE / sub-VP, treating FLOW MATCHING (sub-VP / rectified flow) as the SAME underlying framework under time/noise reparameterization (FM = Euler sampler of the probability-flow ODE; FM noise schedule + DDIM is equivalent to FM Euler). So its core thesis (spacing is one of several knobs; sampling-time emphasis differs from training-time; non-uniform spacing beats uniform) maps directly onto Proteina's linear-OT FM ODE/SDE. The one caveat: the cited concrete mechanism, Align Your Steps, was derived/validated on diffusion SDEs (KLUB via Girsanov, DPM-Solver++/EDM solvers); it is solver- and model-specific and would need to be re-instantiated for Proteina's exact FM sampler rather than dropped in.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: The blog post itself reports NO NFE or speedup (purely conceptual). The credible numbers come from the mechanism it endorses, Align Your Steps (NVIDIA, ICML 2024): training-free schedule optimization giving its largest gains in the few-step regime (N <= 20, examples at 8-10 steps), with consistent FID improvements and ~2x human preference vs hand-crafted schedules at matched step count — i.e. better quality at fixed NFE, or equal quality at fewer NFE. For Proteina (NFE~200), a realistic, defensible expectation is a modest cut (roughly 200 -> ~100-150 steps) from better step PLACEMENT alone, not the aggressive few-step (<=20) cuts AYS shows on images, because protein/topology quality is brittle to under-sampling. No protein-specific number is published — any speedup must be measured empirically on the downstream fold/topology metric.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Yes in principle. Nothing in the thesis is image-specific at the math level — it operates on the logSNR axis common to all (CA-only or otherwise) FM/diffusion samplers. The ONE image-specific argument (low-frequency/perceptual bits matter more, most bits perceptually irrelevant) does NOT transfer to CA coordinates and must be replaced by a protein-appropriate notion of "important noise levels" (e.g. the levels that set global fold/topology, and levels where ODE/SDE error compounds into wrong topology). The actionable, domain-agnostic part — re-allocate sampling steps via logSNR-uniform or analytically optimized spacing instead of uniform — does apply to CA-only backbone generation.
verdict.implementation_cost: LOW for the cheap win, MEDIUM for the optimal one. (1) Cheapest: switch Proteina's sampling-step spacing to logSNR-uniform — Proteina already exposes schedule_mode knobs (uniform/log/power/cos_sch_v_snr/loglinear/edm), so this is mostly a config/one-mapping-function change plus an NFE sweep judged on the downstream template-search/topology metric. Training-free, no checkpoint change. (2) Optimal: port Align Your Steps — re-derive/optimize the step schedule (KLUB) for Proteina's specific linear-OT FM SDE g(t) and Euler solver, then validate. This is a few-hundred-line, solver-specific effort (AYS code is public but written for diffusion DPM-Solver++/EDM image pipelines, not a CA-only FM SDE), so it needs adaptation, not reuse. Both are inference-only.
verdict.quality_cost: No inherent quality cost — the entire premise is to PRESERVE (often improve) quality at fixed or reduced NFE by relocating steps, not removing capability. Risk is empirical: an aggressive step cut or a mis-specified "important range" (e.g. copying the image perceptual-frequency prior, which is wrong for CA coords) could starve the fold-determining noise levels and corrupt topology. Mitigation = treat the downstream fold/topology metric as the gate and sweep, rather than trusting an image-derived schedule. Realistically: modest NFE cut at neutral quality is credible; large cuts (<=20 steps a la AYS images) likely degrade protein topology.
verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and well-known: it is Sander Dieleman's 2024 blog post "Noise schedules considered harmful" (sander.ai, title nods to Dijkstra) — a conceptual essay, not a peer-reviewed paper, but accurately described. All claimed properties verified verbatim: it reframes the noise schedule as one of several interacting knobs (parameterization, loss weighting, timestep distribution p(t), sampling spacing); argues uniform spacing is suboptimal and you should pack steps into more-important / harder-to-predict / error-compounding noise ranges; points to logSNR-uniform spacing and Align Your Steps as the concrete mechanisms; and distinguishes optimal training-time vs sampling-time emphasis. Training-free, no code/NFE/speedup of its own (correct). It TRANSFERS to flow matching cleanly because it is written in unified logSNR terms that subsume VP/VE/sub-VP=flow-matching. For Proteina (CA-only FM, NFE~200): the actionable move is re-spacing sampling steps (logSNR-uniform via existing schedule_mode knobs = low cost; porting AYS to Proteina's FM SDE = medium cost). Credible outcome is a MODEST NFE cut (~200->100-150) at neutral fold/topology quality; the aggressive <=20-step gains AYS shows on images should NOT be assumed for proteins, and the image-specific "perceptual frequency" notion of importance must be replaced by a fold/topology-aware one and validated on the downstream metric. Net: a useful conceptual lens that justifies and motivates the schedule-spacing knobs you already have; the speedup must be measured, not borrowed.

verifiedPapers.title: Dynamical regimes of diffusion models
verifiedPapers.authors: Giulio Biroli, Tony Bonnaire, Valentin de Bortoli, Marc Mézard
verifiedPapers.core_idea: Mean-field / random-matrix analysis of the reverse generative process identifies three regimes: a noise-dominated regime, a SPECIATION transition (the 'gross' / coarse class structure of the data commits via a symmetry-breaking phase transition), and a COLLAPSE transition (trajectory locks onto a memorized datapoint, glass condensation). Key operational result: the speciation time/noise level is computable from the SPECTRUM of the data covariance matrix; collapse time from an 'excess entropy'. This is the foundational physics statement that a generative trajectory commits to a coarse category (analogue of a fold/topology class) at a sharply identifiable noise level, well before the end of sampling.
verifiedPapers.applicable_to_protein_ca: High in principle: the speciation-noise-level analysis is a property of the data distribution (covariance spectrum), so it could be computed directly on the CATH-labeled CA-coordinate dataset to predict the noise level at which fold/topology is decided. Caveat: derived for score-based VP/VE diffusion with Gaussian forward process; transfer to linear OT flow-matching requires re-deriving the noise<->time mapping, but the covariance-spectrum intuition carries over.
verifiedPapers.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (analysis paper; no sampler speedup reported, but directly motivates concentrating NFE around the speciation window and coarsening steps elsewhere)
verifiedPapers.implementation_cost: Medium: compute covariance spectrum of CA-coordinate (or fold-feature) dataset, map to flow-matching time; mostly analytic + linear algebra.
verifiedPapers.notes: Cornerstone for operationalizing a 'fold-commitment noise level'. If speciation occurs at a specific t*, you can use coarse Euler steps before t* and only spend dense NFE across the commitment window — exactly the NFE-reduction strategy our project wants. The covariance-spectrum recipe is computable on our own CA data.

verdict.verdict_summary: REAL and high-credibility. "Dynamical Regimes of Diffusion Models" (Biroli, Bonnaire, de Bortoli, Mezard), peer-reviewed in Nature Communications 15, 9957 (2024); arXiv:2402.18491. All claimed properties verified: three regimes (noise-dominated -> speciation -> collapse); speciation is a symmetry-breaking phase transition that commits the coarse/gross class structure of the data; collapse is a glass-like condensation onto a memorized datapoint. Speciation time is computed from the data covariance SPECTRUM (t_S = (1/2)log(Lambda), Lambda = largest eigenvalue, i.e. top principal component crossing the noise level); collapse time from an 'excess entropy'. CAVEAT 1 (framework): it is a SCORE-BASED / Ornstein-Uhlenbeck variance-preserving DIFFUSION analysis (forward dx=-x dt+dB, backward score SDE). It does NOT mention flow matching; the result is a statement about the diffusion trajectory geometry, not specifically EDM and not flow-matching. CAVEAT 2 (code URL wrong): the claimed repo github.com/tonybonnaire/diffusion_dynamical_regimes does NOT exist. The real public repo is github.com/tbonnair/Dynamical-Regimes-of-Diffusion-Models (MIT, Jupyter+Python, reproduces paper figures). CAVEAT 3 (no sampler result): this is purely an analysis paper. It reports NO sampler, NO NFE reduction, NO speedup. The 'concentrate NFE around the speciation window' application is the requester's inference, not a validated result of the paper.
verdict.credible_nfe_or_speedup: None claimed or demonstrated by the paper. It is an analysis/theory paper with zero sampler or NFE results. Any speedup is a downstream hypothesis you would have to build and validate yourself. The plausible mechanism it motivates: the trajectory commits its coarse category (fold/topology analogue) at a sharply identifiable speciation noise level relatively EARLY, and most of the late trajectory is collapse/refinement toward a specific datapoint. This argues for a NON-UNIFORM step schedule -- dense steps bracketing the speciation window, coarse steps before and after -- which is exactly the kind of thing Proteina's existing schedule_mode/schedule_p knobs can express WITHOUT retraining. But the paper gives no number: it does not tell you how few total steps survive, nor the quality cost. Realistic expectation if pursued: a modest, empirically-tuned step REALLOCATION (not a dramatic NFE cut), with speciation-time t_S computed from the eigenspectrum of the CA-coordinate covariance of a reference backbone set as a principled anchor for WHERE to place dense steps. A 2x naive cut (200->100) is the regime the project already says degrades quality; this paper offers a principled placement heuristic, not a free 2x.
verdict.transfers_to_flow_matching: PARTIAL / requires re-derivation, do not assume it transfers for free. The paper's math is built on the OU variance-preserving forward process and the score-based backward SDE; the speciation criterion Lambda*exp(-2 t_S)=1 is specific to that exponential variance schedule. Proteina is LINEAR optimal-transport flow matching (different interpolant, different time parameterization, different noise schedule). The QUALITATIVE phenomenon -- an early symmetry-breaking moment where coarse class/topology commits, followed by late fine-grained lock-in -- is widely observed and is expected to carry over to flow-matching ODE/SDE sampling (related follow-up and adaptive-schedule works treat diffusion and flow matching as reparameterizations of each other). But the SPECIFIC formula for t_S would need to be re-mapped from the diffusion noise axis to the flow-matching time axis (sigma(t) <-> t correspondence) before the covariance-spectrum prediction is numerically usable. Treat the spectral/excess-entropy formulas as diffusion-specific; treat the three-regime picture as the transferable, motivating idea.
verdict.applies_to_protein_ca: Yes, in principle, and CA-only is actually a convenient case. The analysis is data-agnostic: it only needs the covariance/correlation matrix of the data vectors (here the flattened CA coordinates, dimension 3N for an N-residue backbone) for speciation, and an excess-entropy estimate for collapse. The 'gross structure' that commits at speciation is a natural analogue of fold/topology class, which is exactly the signal the downstream template-search pipeline depends on -- so the framing is well-matched to the goal. Caveats specific to proteins: (1) backbones are SE(3)/rototranslation-equivariant and variable-length, so you must fix an alignment/centering convention before forming the covariance, and the spectrum is over a structured (not iid-pixel) space; (2) sequence-conditioning narrows the target distribution, which shifts where speciation sits versus the unconditional analysis the paper studies; (3) excess-entropy / collapse estimation needs a reference dataset and is the harder quantity to compute reliably.
verdict.implementation_cost: LOW-to-MODERATE and training-free. To USE it as a diagnostic/heuristic: (a) collect CA coordinates of a reference backbone set, build the (centered, length-normalized) covariance, take its top eigenvalue(s) -> get a speciation-region estimate; (b) re-map that noise level onto Proteina's flow-matching time axis; (c) set schedule_mode/schedule_p (existing knobs, no retraining) to place dense Euler steps around the speciation window and coarse steps elsewhere; (d) sweep total NFE down empirically and measure fold/topology quality + template-search hit rate. That is a few days of analysis + sweeps, all inference-side. The HIGH-cost / lower-confidence parts: re-deriving the exact t_S criterion for the OT flow-matching interpolant rather than the OU schedule, and computing a trustworthy excess-entropy/collapse estimate for variable-length SE(3) backbones. The public code (github.com/tbonnair/Dynamical-Regimes-of-Diffusion-Models, MIT) is for reproducing paper figures (Gaussian mixtures, MNIST/CIFAR/ImageNet), NOT a drop-in sampler; you reuse the spectral-analysis idea, not the code.
verdict.quality_cost: Unquantified by the paper -- it reports no sampler and no quality-vs-NFE tradeoff, so any number would be invented. Conceptually: steps placed BEFORE speciation and well AFTER collapse carry little fold-determining information and can be coarsened cheaply; steps spanning the speciation window are where topology commits and must stay dense, so over-coarsening THERE is where you would lose fold/topology fidelity (the exact thing the pipeline cares about). Net expectation: a principled step-reallocation can hold quality roughly constant while trimming wasted steps, but the paper provides no guarantee and no magnitude; the actual quality cost must be measured on Proteina via the downstream template-search metric. Do not present any speedup factor as paper-backed.

allDeduped.title: Distilled Protein Backbone Generation
allDeduped.authors: Liyang Xie, Haoran Zhang, Zhendong Wang, Wesley Tansey, Mingyuan Zhou
allDeduped.venue: arXiv preprint (UT Austin + Memorial Sloan Kettering)
allDeduped.core_idea: Distills Proteina ITSELF into a few-step generator using Score identity Distillation (SiD), adapting SiD to flow matching by deriving the score function from the FM velocity. Key recipe: train multi-step (not 1-step) few-step generators + low sampling temperature + inference-time noise scaling. Tests K in {1,5,8,10,12,16,20} steps.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes - directly. Operates on the exact Proteina CA-only flow-matching backbone model we use; Proteina is rectified flow + SDE solver at 400 steps in this paper.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Distilled 16-step generator BEATS the 400-step Proteina teacher on designability (94.4% vs 94.2%) and novelty, >20x sampling-time reduction. Also works at 8-20 steps; 1-step degrades.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High: requires a SiD distillation training run against Proteina as teacher (two-network SiD setup), plus tuning sampling temperature and inference noise scale. But code targets exactly our model.
allDeduped.notes: THE most relevant paper for this project - it is literally Proteina distillation to ~16 NFE (vs our ~200 default / their 400 baseline) with no quality loss. Confirms our teacher is flow matching + rectified flow + SDE, CA-only. Caveat: needs a distillation training run, not training-free. The finding that multi-step (not 1-step) + inference noise modulation is essential directly informs how aggressively we can cut NFE.

allDeduped.title: DPM-Solver++: Fast Solver for Guided Sampling of Diffusion Probabilistic Models
allDeduped.authors: Cheng Lu, Yuhao Zhou, Fan Bao, Jianfei Chen, Chongxuan Li, Jun Zhu
allDeduped.venue: arXiv preprint (Machine Intelligence Research 2025); widely deployed in diffusers
allDeduped.category: training-free fast ODE solver (data-prediction, multistep, predictor)
allDeduped.core_idea: Reformulates DPM-Solver in DATA (x0) prediction space (more stable under guidance), uses a multistep variant (reuses cached previous model outputs, no extra NFE) and thresholding to stay on the data manifold. Multistep reduces effective step size and instability at low NFE.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes — same mapping as DPM-Solver. The data-prediction (x0) form is convenient for flow matching since velocity, x0, and noise predictions are linearly interchangeable along a linear path. Thresholding (designed for bounded pixel space) is not meaningful for CA coordinates and should be disabled.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: High-quality guided samples in 15-20 steps (vs 100-250 for DDIM); multistep DPM-Solver++(2M) is a standard 20-step workhorse.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low. Available as DPMSolverMultistepScheduler in HuggingFace diffusers; the multistep (2M) variant is the most robust low-NFE training-free option to try first.
allDeduped.notes: Most practical first thing to try: drop-in multistep solver, no retraining, well-tested. Disable thresholding for coordinates. Pair with the SDE variant if Proteina's noise-injection ('sc') quality must be preserved. The diffusers FlowMatchEuler/DPM schedulers already implement the FM<->diffusion schedule rescaling needed.

allDeduped.title: UniPC: A Unified Predictor-Corrector Framework for Fast Sampling of Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Wenliang Zhao, Lujia Bai, Yongming Rao, Jie Zhou, Jiwen Lu
allDeduped.category: training-free fast solver (unified predictor-corrector, arbitrary order)
allDeduped.core_idea: Adds a Unified Corrector (UniC) that can be appended after ANY existing solver step to raise accuracy order with NO extra NFE (reuses the just-computed model output), plus a Unified Predictor (UniP) of arbitrary order. Generalizes DPM-Solver++/DEIS into one model-agnostic predictor-corrector.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes — same semilinear-ODE mapping as DPM-Solver/DEIS. Predictor-corrector is attractive because the corrector improves order for free (important when each Proteina NFE is expensive). Untested on CA-only structures.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 3.87 FID (CIFAR-10 uncond.) and 7.51 FID (ImageNet 256, cond.) at NFE=10; strong gains specifically in the 5-10 NFE regime.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low. UniPCMultistepScheduler in diffusers; the free corrector makes it often the best training-free choice at NFE<=10.
allDeduped.notes: Likely the strongest training-free option in the 5-15 NFE band our project targets, because the corrector buys an accuracy order without a 2x NFE cost. Same FM-reparameterization caveat applies. Already FM-compatible in diffusers.

allDeduped.title: Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion-Based Generative Models (EDM)
allDeduped.authors: Tero Karras, Miika Aittala, Timo Aila, Samuli Laine
allDeduped.category: training-free sampler (Heun 2nd-order) + noise-schedule/discretization design + stochastic sampler
allDeduped.core_idea: Disentangles solver, time-discretization, preconditioning and noise schedule. Provides a deterministic Heun 2nd-order ODE sampler (1 extra eval per step gives 2nd-order accuracy) and a tunable STOCHASTIC sampler (churn S_churn/S_min/S_max/S_noise) that adds and removes noise — directly analogous to Proteina's SDE 'sc' noise injection. Also gives the EDM time-discretization (the 'edm' schedule_mode already in our codebase).
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Strong fit. Heun is purely a solver (no pixel assumptions) and is the natural training-free upgrade from Euler for the FM ODE. The EDM stochastic sampler is the cleanest principled framework for keeping a tunable noise term while cutting steps — relevant since naively cutting Proteina's stochastic sampler to 100 steps degrades quality. EDM-style sigma/time discretization is already exposed as schedule_mode='edm'.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: SOTA FID 1.79 (CIFAR-10) at 35 NFE; Heun reaches the same FID as Euler with substantially fewer steps (roughly halves step count for matched quality).
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low. Heun = a few lines (predictor + correction using a 2nd eval); EDM stochastic churn is a small, well-documented loop. No model changes.
allDeduped.notes: Most directly aligned with our schedule knobs (edm schedule_mode, g(t) noise, schedule_p exponent) and our need to preserve a stochastic sampler. Heun trades 2 NFE/step for 2nd-order accuracy — net win when Euler needs many small steps. Karras = same NVIDIA lineage as Proteina, so the discretization conventions match. Best conceptual anchor for tuning our existing knobs training-free.

allDeduped.title: Leveraging Previous Steps: A Training-free Fast Solver for Flow Diffusion (FlowSolver)
allDeduped.authors: Kaiyu Song, Hanjiang Lai
allDeduped.category: training-free fast solver designed FOR flow matching (multistep / polynomial Taylor)
allDeduped.core_idea: Adapts the DPM-Solver/DEIS multistep idea natively to FLOW-MATCHING velocity-prediction models: caches previous solver steps and uses polynomial interpolation to approximate the high-order Taylor derivatives of the velocity field, raising solver order without extra NFE or retraining. Built specifically for the linear/flow ODE rather than the score ODE.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Most directly transferable in principle — it already assumes a velocity-field FM model (exactly Proteina's parameterization), so no noise/score reparameterization needed. Demonstrated only on image FM models; CA-only protein application untested but the math carries over directly.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: FID-30K 13.79->6.75 (CIFAR-10) and 46.64->19.49 (LSUN-Church) at NFE=10 vs baseline Euler at equal NFE.
allDeduped.public_code_url: not located (no public repo found as of search)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium. Conceptually simple (cache + polynomial extrapolation of velocity) and matches our model's native parameterization, but no released code found, so likely a from-paper reimplementation.
allDeduped.notes: Highest conceptual fit: a training-free multistep solver written for velocity-prediction FM ODEs, so it sidesteps the score/diffusion reparameterization caveat that DPM-Solver/DEIS/UniPC carry. Only addresses the deterministic ODE; combine with EDM-churn or Restart to retain Proteina's stochastic sampler. Reimplementation needed.

allDeduped.title: Align Your Steps: Optimizing Sampling Schedules in Diffusion Models (AYS)
allDeduped.authors: Amirmojtaba Sabour, Sanja Fidler, Karsten Kreis (NVIDIA)
allDeduped.core_idea: Uses stochastic calculus (KL-divergence upper bound on the discretization error) to compute the OPTIMAL set of timesteps for a GIVEN solver, model, and dataset — replacing hand-crafted uniform/log/EDM schedules. Solver-specific and dataset-specific optimal schedules.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Directly relevant to our schedule_mode/schedule_p knobs: AYS is the principled way to set those discretization points instead of guessing among uniform/log/power/cos/edm. The KL-bound machinery applies to the FM ODE/SDE; would need to be derived/fit for the CA-only Proteina noise schedule, but no retraining.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Consistent quality gains at fixed low NFE (~10) across Euler/DPM/EDM samplers on CIFAR-10/FFHQ/ImageNet; text-to-image outputs preferred ~2x in user study at equal steps.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium. Precomputed schedules are dataset-specific; deriving an AYS schedule for our CA Proteina model requires running their optimization (a one-time offline cost), but inference is then free.
allDeduped.notes: Squarely targets the project's exposed knobs (schedule_mode, schedule_p) and the observation that naive uniform step-cutting degrades quality. Same NVIDIA group as Proteina, so conventions align. Best 'spend offline compute once, then sample cheaply' lever; complementary to (stackable with) any high-order solver above.

allDeduped.title: Optimal Stepsize for Diffusion Sampling (OSS)
allDeduped.authors: Jianning Pei, Han Hu, Shuyang Gu
allDeduped.category: Optimal stepsize via dynamic programming distilled from reference trajectories
allDeduped.core_idea: Casts stepsize selection as recursive error minimization with optimal substructure and solves it with dynamic programming to distill a 'theoretically optimal' schedule from reference (teacher) trajectories. Unlike GITS's fixed local-cost matrix, OSS aims at the global discretization error. Resulting schedules are reported robust across architectures, ODE solvers, and noise schedules.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising. 'Distillation' here means distilling a SCHEDULE from reference trajectories, not retraining the base network — the base model is frozen. The DP/optimal-substructure formulation is solver- and noise-schedule-agnostic and explicitly claimed robust across noise schedules, so adapting to an OT flow-matching ODE is plausible: generate high-NFE reference CA trajectories with Proteina, then run the DP to extract a few-step schedule. Need to confirm the error metric used is meaningful in CA-coordinate space.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: ~10x acceleration in text-to-image while preserving 99.4% of GenEval performance; emphasizes very-low-NFE schedules.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low-moderate. Generate reference trajectories once, run DP. Frozen base model.
allDeduped.notes: The current state-of-the-art 'optimal time-schedule search' for a fixed model, improving on GITS by targeting global rather than local error, with clean public code and claimed cross-noise-schedule robustness. Top candidate alongside GITS. The word 'distillation' can mislead — it is schedule distillation, NOT model distillation, so it stays training-free for our purposes.

allDeduped.title: Proteina: Scaling Flow-based Protein Structure Generative Models (own reported sampling settings)
allDeduped.authors: Geffner, Didi, Zhang, Reidenbach, Cao, Yim, Geiger, Dallago, Kucukbenli, Vahdat, Kreis et al. (NVIDIA)
allDeduped.core_idea: This is OUR base model. Reported sampling recipe: linear/OT flow-matching ODE simulated with a first-order Euler integrator; default deployment uses the STOCHASTIC SDE sampler (sampling_mode='sc', score-based with Langevin term) at ~400 integration steps (NFE=400), with the deterministic ODE ('vf') as an alternative. Key tunable knobs exposed: sc_scale_noise (noise/temperature, default 0.35-0.45; trades designability vs diversity), schedule_mode=log, schedule_p=2.0, and the non-uniform training/inference t-distribution p(t)=0.02*U(0,1)+0.98*Beta(1.9,1.0). The gamma noise-injection scale controls SDE-vs-ODE marginal matching (gamma=1 matches ODE distribution). Appendix L ablates t-sampling distributions and g(t) stochasticity schedules. Establishes the baseline NFE we must cut.
allDeduped.category: Base model / sampler baseline (the target to accelerate)
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes - this IS the CA-only flow-matching model we are accelerating.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Default ~400 NFE (SDE/Euler). Naively dropping to ~100 degrades quality.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: None - already in our codebase; just the reference point.
allDeduped.notes: Schedule/temperature knobs (schedule_mode, schedule_p, sc_scale_noise, gamma) are the first, training-free lever to try for fewer steps before any distillation.

allDeduped.title: Scaling Rectified Flow Transformers for High-Resolution Image Synthesis (Stable Diffusion 3)
allDeduped.authors: Patrick Esser, Sumith Kulal, Andreas Blattmann, et al. (Stability AI)
allDeduped.core_idea: Two distinct contributions. (1) TRAINING: compares non-uniform timestep-sampling densities for rectified-flow training - uniform, logit-normal(m,s), mode, cosmap - and finds logit-normal centered near t=0 (lognorm(0.00,1.00)) best (emphasizes mid-trajectory). (2) SAMPLING (training-free): a time-SHIFT reparameterization t' = a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) with shift a=sqrt(m/n) scaled by resolution (a=3 at 1024px); larger a pushes steps toward the high-noise end. The shift is the canonical FM analog of EDM's rho.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Highly - this is a NATIVE flow-matching (rectified-flow) formulation, not score/diffusion, so it transfers far more cleanly to our linear-OT FM ODE/SDE than diffusion-sigma works. The sampling-time shift is a one-line training-free knob directly usable on Proteina's Euler integrator. The shift magnitude is a function of effective dimensionality (resolution for images -> roughly sequence length / token count for CA backbones), so it should be tuned per protein length.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Shift enables high-quality few-step sampling at higher resolutions; paper's focus is quality-at-fixed-budget rather than a single headline NFE number. The logit-normal training schedule outperforms uniform-RF and 61 ablated diffusion/FM formulations.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Trivial for the sampling shift (1 line on the time grid; equivalent to a specific schedule_p/loglinear setting). The logit-normal training change only matters if we ever retrain/distill.
allDeduped.notes: Most directly on-paradigm reference (rectified flow). The t'=a*t/(1+(a-1)*t) shift is the precise FM time-schedule equivalent of EDM-rho and is exactly the kind of knob to sweep on Proteina. Note the resolution->shift scaling means the optimal shift likely depends on backbone length.

allDeduped.title: Flows, straight but not so fast: Exploring the Design Space of Rectified Flows in Protein Design
allDeduped.authors: Junhua Chen, Simon Mathis, Charles Harris, Kieran Didi, Pietro Lio
allDeduped.category: flow-matching inference schedule + ReFlow for protein backbones
allDeduped.core_idea: Systematic study of the low-NFE design space for SE(3) flow-matching protein backbone models (FoldFlow-OT, QFlow). Two separable findings: (A) TRAINING-FREE - a 'Noise-Focused' time discretization (finer steps near t=0, the noise end, where SO(3) trajectory curvature is highest) beats uniform/data-focused/edge-focused for both models; inference annealing (velocity scaling, lowering effective temperature) trades designability vs diversity. (B) ReFlow rectification TRAINING is needed for the largest low-NFE gains and is sensitive to coupling-generation direction and annealing; image-domain ReFlow tricks do NOT transfer to proteins.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Directly - same domain (SE(3)/frame flow-matching protein backbones). The Noise-Focused discretization and annealing findings are training-free and immediately testable on Proteina. Caveat: their models are frame-based (Ca,C,N,O), whereas Proteina is CA-only, and 'noise-focused' (steps near t=0) is the OPPOSITE end from EDM/AYS 'low-noise/near-data' concentration because of the FM time convention - verify the time direction carefully before porting.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: FoldFlow-OT @15 NFE: designability 0.584->0.664 (rectified); QFlow @20 NFE: 0.528->0.780, scRMSD 1.74A. High-NFE (50-100) performance DEGRADES after rectification - rectification is a low-NFE-only win.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low for the training-free discretization/annealing sweep (closest direct precedent for what we want); high if we pursue ReFlow rectification (needs retraining + careful coupling/annealing).
allDeduped.notes: The single most on-target paper: same modality, same goal (cut NFE without losing designability), and it isolates which schedule choices are training-free vs need retraining. Strongly recommend reproducing their Noise-Focused-vs-uniform discretization + annealing sweep on Proteina first. Watch the t-direction convention mismatch.

allDeduped.title: On the Trajectory Regularity of ODE-based Diffusion Sampling (GITS: Geometry-Inspired Time Scheduling)
allDeduped.authors: Defang Chen, Zhenyu Zhou, Can Wang, Chunhua Shen, Siwei Lyu
allDeduped.category: Geometry/curvature-based step placement via dynamic programming
allDeduped.core_idea: Observes that ODE sampling trajectories have strong geometric regularity (a 'boomerang' shape). GITS allocates larger steps where trajectory curvature is small and smaller steps where curvature is large, formulating discretization as a shortest-path problem that minimizes accumulated local truncation error, solved by dynamic programming over a candidate time grid (one hyperparameter 'coeff'). Minimal modification to any ODE solver, negligible compute, strong gains at 5-10 NFE.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising. GITS is solver-agnostic and only needs the model's own trajectory geometry, which exists equally for a flow-matching ODE. The DP over a candidate grid to minimize accumulated local truncation error transfers cleanly; we would build the cost matrix by running a handful of reference trajectories from our Proteina CA model and applying the same DP. No image-specific assumptions in the core algorithm. Main caveat: cost matrix uses a fixed reference (deviates from true global-error optimum), and the 'boomerang' regularity should be verified for CA-coordinate trajectories.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Targets 5-10 NFE; reported superior FID vs uniform/heuristic schedules in the few-step regime at negligible added cost.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low. Build a cost matrix from reference trajectories + run a small DP; integrates into existing Euler/Heun samplers with little code.
allDeduped.public_code_url: https://github.com/zju-pi/diff-sampler (gits-main); also a GITS scheduler PR in ComfyUI
allDeduped.notes: Highly transferable and training-free, with public code. The DP-over-trajectory-geometry idea is one of the cleanest few-step schedule-search methods and does not assume score/EDM in its core, making it a strong direct candidate for our flow-matching CA model. Note GITS minimizes accumulated LOCAL error (not strictly global) — OSS below addresses that.

allDeduped.title: Learning to Discretize Denoising Diffusion ODEs (LD3)
allDeduped.authors: Vinh Tong, Trung-Dung Hoang, Anji Liu, Guy Van den Broeck, Mathias Niepert
allDeduped.core_idea: Treats the discretization time points as trainable parameters and optimizes them by backpropagating through a differentiable ODE-solving pipeline to directly minimize global truncation error (teacher high-NFE vs student few-NFE). Introduces a surrogate loss L_soft to fix underfitting from direct global-error optimization, proven close to the true distillation objective. Does NOT retrain the diffusion model.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising and arguably the cleanest fit conceptually. The only thing 'trained' is a small set of timesteps (a handful of scalars) via differentiable ODE solving; the base velocity network is frozen. Since our Proteina sampler is already an Euler-integrated ODE/SDE, making it differentiable w.r.t. the time grid is mechanically straightforward, and the global-truncation-error objective is domain-agnostic (works on CA coordinates as the state). Very cheap (5-10 min training reported on images). Main work: implement the differentiable solver pass and L_soft surrogate for the FM ODE.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Matches/beats baselines at 10 NFE (FID 2.38 CIFAR10, 2.27 AFHQv2 at 10 NFE); learns the schedule in only 5-10 minutes of training on a single GPU.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Moderate. Needs a differentiable ODE-solver pass + surrogate loss, but optimizes only ~N timestep scalars; very cheap wall-clock.
allDeduped.notes: Best 'learning-to-discretize' entry: directly minimizes global truncation error, frozen base model, tiny trainable parameter count, minutes of training, solver-agnostic. Strong direct candidate — the FM ODE just becomes the differentiable pipeline. Listed as requires_training=true only because the few timestep scalars are learned (not the model).

allDeduped.title: Differentiable Solver Search for Fast Diffusion Sampling
allDeduped.authors: Shuai Wang, Zexian Li, Qipeng Zhang, Tianhui Song, Xubin Li, Tiezheng Ge, Bo Zheng, Limin Wang
allDeduped.category: Co-optimization of time-step schedule AND solver coefficients (differentiable search)
allDeduped.core_idea: Shows standard t-related Lagrange interpolation is suboptimal and identifies a compact search space of {time steps, solver coefficients}. Uses a differentiable search to jointly find the optimal schedule and solver coefficients on a frozen pretrained model, without harming pretraining. The searched solver+schedule transfers across architectures, resolutions, and model sizes.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Strong fit. Explicitly validated on rectified-flow / flow-matching backbones (SiT-XL/2, FlowDCN-XL/2) in addition to DDPM/DiT — directly relevant since Proteina is flow-matching. Only a few solver coefficients + timesteps are searched (base model frozen), and results transfer across model sizes/resolutions, suggesting a search done on a calibration set could yield a reusable few-step schedule+solver for our CA model. Caveat: validated on image/latent generation; CA-coordinate state and SDE (stochastic) sampling would need testing; method is most natural for the ODE/deterministic case.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 10-step FID 2.40 (SiT-XL/2, rectified flow) and 2.35 (FlowDCN-XL/2) on ImageNet-256, 2.33 (DiT-XL/2) — i.e. high quality at NFE=10.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Moderate. Differentiable search over a small parameter set on a calibration set; base model frozen. More involved than a pure schedule search since it also tunes solver coefficients.
allDeduped.public_code_url: Referenced in paper / OpenReview (https://openreview.net/forum?id=FB84Wkn3Xp); repo from Nanjing University + Alibaba
allDeduped.notes: Among the most directly applicable: explicitly works on flow-matching (rectified-flow) models and co-optimizes WHERE the steps go plus the solver, hitting NFE=10 at high quality. If we are willing to also tune solver coefficients (not just timesteps) this is arguably the best-matched method for our FM CA setting. Listed requires_training=true (small coefficient/timestep search, model frozen).

allDeduped.title: Diffusion is spectral autoregression
allDeduped.authors: Sander Dieleman
allDeduped.core_idea: Argues that in the visual domain diffusion sampling is approximate autoregression in the spatial-frequency domain. Because natural-image spectra follow a power law (~f^-alpha) while Gaussian noise is spectrally flat, increasing noise acts as a frequency-dependent low-pass filter: at HIGH noise only low frequencies (coarse/global structure) survive and are generated first; at LOW noise only high frequencies (fine detail) remain to be reconstructed. Implies the noise level directly indexes which spatial scale the model is committing, and that perceptually important coarse structure is decided in the mid-to-high-noise regime.
allDeduped.category: Spectral / coarse-to-fine analysis of noise levels
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Partial. The power-law-vs-flat-noise argument is general to any signal with a non-flat power spectrum. CA-only protein backbones are 1D chains of 3D points; their spectral structure (low-frequency = overall fold/radius-of-gyration, high-frequency = local zig-zag) plausibly follows the same logic, but the paper itself is image/audio-only, so transfer is by analogy not demonstrated.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None (conceptual)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low (conceptual framing to motivate spending more integration steps in the coarse/high-noise regime that sets topology)
allDeduped.notes: Foundational primary source for THIS angle. Directly supports the hypothesis that global fold/topology is decided at high noise and that step budget should be skewed accordingly. Pairs with Falck's rebuttal below for balance.

allDeduped.title: Noise schedules considered harmful
allDeduped.authors: Sander Dieleman
allDeduped.core_idea: Reframes the 'noise schedule' as just one of several knobs (model parameterization, loss weighting, timestep-sampling distribution, sampling spacing) that jointly decide how much capacity/compute each noise level gets. Key actionable claim: in noise-level ranges that are MORE important (perceptually relevant low frequencies / harder-to-predict levels, and levels where errors compound) you should space sampling steps closer together to evaluate the model more there. Argues uniform spacing is suboptimal and points to logSNR-uniform spacing and analytic step-optimization (Align Your Steps) as better. Also notes optimal training-time vs sampling-time emphasis differ.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes, in principle. The argument is solver- and modality-agnostic: identify which noise levels are most error-prone / most decisive for the downstream metric (here fold topology) and concentrate steps there. Directly motivates a non-uniform Euler step schedule for our flow ODE/SDE.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None directly; cites Align Your Steps / EDM as the concrete mechanisms
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low conceptual; the operational version is just choosing a non-uniform schedule_mode/schedule_p (knobs we already expose).
allDeduped.notes: Most directly actionable conceptual source for our project: it tells us the right lever is WHERE to place the limited Euler steps, not the count alone, and that the placement should follow noise-level importance (which for us = fold-setting mid/high noise).

allDeduped.title: Dynamical regimes of diffusion models
allDeduped.authors: Giulio Biroli, Tony Bonnaire, Valentin de Bortoli, Marc Mézard
allDeduped.core_idea: Mean-field / random-matrix analysis of the reverse generative process identifies three regimes: a noise-dominated regime, a SPECIATION transition (the 'gross' / coarse class structure of the data commits via a symmetry-breaking phase transition), and a COLLAPSE transition (trajectory locks onto a memorized datapoint, glass condensation). Key operational result: the speciation time/noise level is computable from the SPECTRUM of the data covariance matrix; collapse time from an 'excess entropy'. This is the foundational physics statement that a generative trajectory commits to a coarse category (analogue of a fold/topology class) at a sharply identifiable noise level, well before the end of sampling.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: High in principle: the speciation-noise-level analysis is a property of the data distribution (covariance spectrum), so it could be computed directly on the CATH-labeled CA-coordinate dataset to predict the noise level at which fold/topology is decided. Caveat: derived for score-based VP/VE diffusion with Gaussian forward process; transfer to linear OT flow-matching requires re-deriving the noise<->time mapping, but the covariance-spectrum intuition carries over.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (analysis paper; no sampler speedup reported, but directly motivates concentrating NFE around the speciation window and coarsening steps elsewhere)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium: compute covariance spectrum of CA-coordinate (or fold-feature) dataset, map to flow-matching time; mostly analytic + linear algebra.
allDeduped.notes: Cornerstone for operationalizing a 'fold-commitment noise level'. If speciation occurs at a specific t*, you can use coarse Euler steps before t* and only spend dense NFE across the commitment window — exactly the NFE-reduction strategy our project wants. The covariance-spectrum recipe is computable on our own CA data.

allDeduped.title: A Phase Transition in Diffusion Models Reveals the Hierarchical Nature of Data
allDeduped.authors: Antonio Sclocchi, Alessandro Favero, Matthieu Wyart
allDeduped.category: statistical-physics formulation: forward-backward (= fork) experiment + sharp class-flip transition
allDeduped.core_idea: Runs the fork/commitment experiment under the name 'forward-backward experiment': noise a real image up to level t, then denoise it MANY times with different noise. Finds a SHARP phase transition at a threshold t* — above t* the CLASS suddenly changes (high-level feature destroyed), below t* the class is preserved; low-level details instead change smoothly across all t. So t* is precisely the noise level where the category is committed. Explained with a hierarchical (Random Hierarchy Model) generative model of data; transition becomes sharp in the high-dimensional limit. Validated on class-unconditional ImageNet diffusion.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Most directly transferable framing for proteins: protein structure is hierarchical/compositional (residues to motifs to topology), matching their data model. The forward-backward protocol maps 1:1 onto Proteina: noise a CA backbone to t, denoise repeatedly, find t* where the FOLD flips. t* is the operational boundary for where you must keep fine integration vs where you can take big steps. Strong fit.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (analysis of where commitment happens)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low — forward-backward branching + a fold classifier; same machinery as the DDPM diagnostic
allDeduped.notes: Diffusion-specific derivation, but the forward-backward (fork) measurement and the 'class flips sharply, details flow smoothly' finding are exactly Track C and transfer cleanly to flow-matching CA sampling.

allDeduped.title: Critical windows: non-asymptotic theory for feature emergence in diffusion models
allDeduped.authors: Marvin Li, Sitan Chen
allDeduped.category: rigorous theory of category-commitment / critical-window definition
allDeduped.core_idea: Formalizes the phenomenon the prompt is about: a 'critical window' is a narrow time interval during sampling in which a particular feature (image CLASS, background color, etc.) emerges/commits. They define it operationally via exactly the fork experiment — branch the reverse process at time t and measure the probability the final sample lands in a given feature-group as a function of t; the window is where that probability transitions sharply. Proves that for data = mixture of strongly log-concave densities, the window is bounded by inter-group vs intra-group separation, and interprets diffusion as a HIERARCHICAL sampler that 'progressively decides output features over a discrete sequence of times.' Validates on Gaussian mixtures and Stable Diffusion (also pitched as a fairness/privacy diagnostic).
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Gives the theory that justifies treating fold-commitment as happening in a narrow t-window. The log-concave-mixture model is a reasonable caricature of 'protein backbone clusters by fold' in CA space; tells you to spend NFE inside the critical window and coarsen elsewhere. Bounds are score/diffusion-derived but the structural statement (commitment is windowed, set by group separation) is sampler-agnostic.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (theory + diagnostic)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low to use as a diagnostic lens; the theorems are for analysis, not code
allDeduped.notes: The single most on-point THEORY paper for this angle. Stated for diffusion/score; does not explicitly cover flow matching, but the definition (branch-and-measure-commitment) is directly usable on the OT flow-matching sampler.

allDeduped.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) — §4.3 'Latent structure and reverse process stochasticity' + Figs 7 & 9
allDeduped.authors: Jonathan Ho, Ajay Jain, Pieter Abbeel
allDeduped.core_idea: This is the original, exact experiment the prompt is asking for. They run ONE reverse chain from the prior, then at an intermediate timestep SPLIT/FORK it into multiple completions that share x_t but get independent downstream noise. Verbatim: 'we run a single reverse chain ... At the intermediate timesteps, the chain is split to sample multiple images. When the chain is split after the prior draw at x_{T=1000}, the samples differ significantly. However, when the chain is split after more steps, samples share high-level attributes like gender, hair color, eyewear, saturation, pose and facial expression. This indicates that intermediate latents like x_750 encode these attributes, despite their imperceptibility.' Fig 7 shows the branched completions; Fig 9 is the coarse-to-fine version (vary #steps before latent mixing). This empirically locates the noise level where the CATEGORY/high-level identity is locked in but details still vary.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Directly portable as a DIAGNOSTIC. Replace 'gender/hair' with 'fold/topology/CATH class' and 'pixels' with CA coordinates: fork the Proteina flow-matching ODE/SDE at candidate t, sample K continuations per branch, measure when topology (TM-score / contact-map class) stops changing across seeds. This is the cheap, model-agnostic experiment that tells you the lowest-NFE schedule that still preserves the fold the template-search pipeline needs.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (diagnostic, not a sampler)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low — branching is a few lines around the existing sampler loop; only needs a topology/fold classifier (TM-align or contact map) as the 'category' readout
allDeduped.notes: The literal source of the branch-from-shared-state experiment. Image diffusion, score/DDPM ancestral sampler, but the experimental protocol is sampler-agnostic and transfers verbatim to flow-matching CA sampling.

allDeduped.title: The Entropic Signature of Class Speciation in Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Florian Handke, Dejan Stančević, Felix Koulischer, Thomas Demeester, Luca Ambrogioni
allDeduped.core_idea: Training-free diagnostic: track the class-conditional entropy of a latent semantic variable given the noisy state along the reverse trajectory. The entropy drop reliably marks the narrow regime where samples transition from semantic ambiguity to class commitment (speciation). By restricting entropy to semantic PARTITIONS, it resolves decisions at MULTIPLE levels of abstraction (e.g. coarse class then sub-class) — directly analogous to CATH C -> A -> T hierarchy. Validated on EDM2-XS and Stable Diffusion 1.5.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: High: this is the most directly operational recipe for OUR question. Plug a CA-only fold classifier (GearNet / TM-align-to-CATH-representative) as the 'class' label, run partial samples at each noise level, classify, and compute conditional entropy to find the exact flow-matching time at which fold/topology commits — and do it hierarchically (C then A then T). Method is sampler-agnostic (just needs noisy states + a classifier), so it transfers cleanly to flow-matching ODE/SDE.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (diagnostic; identifies the window you can exploit to save NFE)
allDeduped.public_code_url: Not confirmed in listing (check arXiv page)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low-medium: needs a fold classifier (we already have GearNet-based one from Proteina) + per-noise-level entropy estimation. Reuses existing inference.
allDeduped.notes: Most actionable for building OUR 'fold-commitment classifier' experiment. The hierarchical-partition entropy maps onto CATH C/A/T levels. Combine with Proteina's GearNet classifier to empirically locate the commitment t* on our actual model.

allDeduped.title: Mean Flows for One-step Generative Modeling (MeanFlow)
allDeduped.authors: Zhengyang Geng, Mingyang Deng, Xingjian Bai, J. Zico Kolter, Kaiming He
allDeduped.core_idea: Models the AVERAGE velocity over an interval (vs instantaneous velocity in standard FM) and trains via the MeanFlow identity linking average and instantaneous velocity (a differential identity enforced with JVP). One network predicts the displacement for an arbitrary (t,r) jump, enabling 1-step sampling.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible but unproven on protein CA. Flow-matching native and architecture-agnostic, so portable to Proteina's CA velocity net, but no protein results exist; would require training from scratch or as a teacher-free objective.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1-NFE FID 3.43 on ImageNet 256x256 from scratch; strong 2-NFE. Closes most of the gap to multi-step models.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium-High: requires (re)training with the MeanFlow objective (JVP / average-velocity identity); no teacher needed, but a from-scratch or full fine-tune training run on our data is required.
allDeduped.public_code_url: Official code not released by authors at abstract; unofficial: https://github.com/haidog-yaqub/MeanFlow and https://github.com/noamelata/MeanFlow
allDeduped.notes: Best 'teacher-free' flow-matching-native option - no pretrained teacher or distillation pair, single-stage training. Strong conceptual fit for cutting Proteina to 1-4 NFE, but unverified on protein backbones and needs a training run. Could be combined with our existing schedule knobs.

allDeduped.title: One Step Diffusion via Shortcut Models
allDeduped.authors: Kevin Frans, Danijar Hafner, Sergey Levine, Pieter Abbeel
allDeduped.category: training-based few-step (self-consistency, single network, single stage)
allDeduped.core_idea: Conditions the velocity network on the desired STEP SIZE d in addition to (x,t), and enforces self-consistency: one step of size 2d must equal two chained steps of size d. Trained in a single phase (no separate teacher) by mixing standard FM loss with the self-consistency target; supports any step budget at inference.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible. Built on flow matching / rectified-flow ODE; the only architectural change is adding a step-size conditioning input to Proteina's net, then retraining/fine-tuning. No protein results yet.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: High-quality 1-step generation on images; flexible 1- to many-step at inference from a single trained model.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium: add step-size embedding to the network and run a single-stage training/fine-tune with the consistency term. No teacher network. Variable step budget at inference is a nice fit for our pipeline.
allDeduped.notes: Strong fit because it is flow-matching native, single-stage, and yields ONE model usable across NFE budgets (matches our 'tunable schedule knobs' desire). Adding step-size conditioning to Proteina is a modest change. Self-distillation/bootstrapping can be unstable at very low steps.

allDeduped.title: Consistency Flow Matching: Defining Straight Flows with Velocity Consistency (Consistency-FM)
allDeduped.authors: Ling Yang, Zixiang Zhang, Zhilong Zhang, Xingchao Liu, Minkai Xu, Wentao Zhang, Chenlin Meng, Stefano Ermon, Bin Cui
allDeduped.core_idea: Enforces self-consistency directly in the VELOCITY field of a flow-matching model: defines straight-line flows from different start times to the same endpoint and constrains their velocities to be consistent. Uses multi-segment (piecewise-linear) training to trade quality vs steps.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible. Explicitly a flow-matching extension; supports both from-scratch training and distillation from a pretrained FM model (i.e., could distill Proteina). No protein results.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 2-NFE FID 5.34 on CIFAR-10 (beats Consistency Models 5.83); ~4.4x faster convergence than CM, 1.7x vs rectified flow.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium: can be applied as a distillation loss on top of pretrained Proteina, or trained from scratch; multi-segment scheme adds some tuning.
allDeduped.notes: Flow-matching native and offers a distillation path from our existing Proteina checkpoint (cheaper than scratch). Velocity-space consistency aligns with how Proteina parameterizes its model. Results shown only on small image benchmarks at modest FID.

allDeduped.title: How to Build a Consistency Model: Learning Flow Maps via Self-Distillation
allDeduped.authors: Nicholas M. Boffi, Michael S. Albergo, Eric Vanden-Eijnden
allDeduped.core_idea: Systematic framework deriving flow-map (consistency-model) training from the velocity field via three families - Eulerian, Lagrangian, Progressive - using self-distillation that eliminates the need for a pretrained teacher. Finds Lagrangian methods (no spatial derivatives, no small-step bootstrapping) most stable.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible/principled. General for any flow or diffusion model including FM; provides recipes that could be instantiated on Proteina's CA velocity field. No protein experiments.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Few-step (1-4) generation; exact NFE/FID not surfaced in abstract. Emphasis is stability/quality of flow-map training rather than a single headline number.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium: requires a flow-map training run on our model; the Lagrangian variant avoids JVP/spatial-derivative instabilities, easing implementation. No teacher needed.
allDeduped.notes: Best 'reference manual' for choosing a stable flow-map objective on a flow-matching model. Pairs naturally with Boffi's earlier Flow Map Matching theory. Lagrangian self-distillation is the recommended starting point for adapting to Proteina; theory-forward, no protein validation.

allDeduped.title: Align Your Flow: Scaling Continuous-Time Flow Map Distillation
allDeduped.authors: Amirmojtaba Sabour, Sanja Fidler, Karsten Kreis
allDeduped.core_idea: Introduces two continuous-time objectives (Eulerian + Lagrangian Flow Map Distillation) that generalize consistency and flow-matching objectives, training a single flow map connecting ANY two noise levels and remaining effective across all step counts. Adds training techniques + optional adversarial finetuning.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible. Designed to distill flow/diffusion models; the continuous-time flow-map objective transfers to Proteina's FM-SDE setup. From the same lab as Proteina (NVIDIA/Kreis), so conventions align. No protein results.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: SOTA few-step generation on ImageNet 64x64 and 512x512; text-to-image flow maps beat all non-adversarial few-step samplers. Effective from 1 to many steps.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium-High: continuous-time flow-map distillation from pretrained Proteina; quality robust across step counts (good for tunable NFE). Adversarial finetune optional.
allDeduped.public_code_url: Code referenced by authors (NVIDIA); check project page / NVlabs (not confirmed at fetch time)
allDeduped.notes: Strong candidate: same NVIDIA group as Proteina, flow-map distillation that stays good at every NFE (1-step up), can distill our existing checkpoint. Most directly comparable architecture lineage. Verify code release before committing.

allDeduped.title: InstaFlow: One Step is Enough (Rectified Flow + Reflow)
allDeduped.authors: Xingchao Liu, Xiwen Zhang, Jianzhu Ma, Jian Peng, Qiang Liu
allDeduped.venue: ICLR 2024 (UT Austin); builds on Rectified Flow, ICLR 2023
allDeduped.core_idea: Reflow iteratively re-couples noise-data pairs to STRAIGHTEN the rectified-flow ODE trajectories, after which a single Euler step suffices; a final distillation step yields a 1-step generator. Reflow is the enabler - straight paths make few-step Euler accurate.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible and conceptually aligned - Proteina IS rectified flow, so reflow is directly applicable to straighten its CA-coordinate trajectories and cut NFE. Requires generating noise-sample pairs from the teacher and retraining. No protein results.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1-step text-to-image in ~0.09s; 2-rectified-flow enables accurate few-step Euler before distillation.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium-High: reflow needs a dataset of teacher-generated (noise, sample) couplings + retraining; optional final distillation. Pure-reflow (no distill) already reduces NFE.
allDeduped.notes: Directly relevant because Proteina is rectified flow - reflow is the matched straightening technique and can be applied without changing the architecture (just re-couple + retrain). Even one reflow round can let us drop to fewer Euler steps before any aggressive distillation. Diversity can drop with repeated reflow; watch fold diversity.

allDeduped.title: DMD / DMD2: Distribution Matching Distillation for Fast Synthesis
allDeduped.authors: Tianwei Yin, Michael Gharbi, Richard Zhang, Eli Shechtman, Fredo Durand, William T. Freeman, Taesung Park (DMD2)
allDeduped.core_idea: Trains a 1-step generator to match the teacher's DISTRIBUTION by minimizing an approximate KL whose gradient is the difference of two score functions (real vs fake critic). DMD2 removes the regression loss/dataset, adds a two-time-scale fake-critic update and a GAN loss for stability and scale.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible: this is the same family as SiD (the protein-distillation paper uses SiD, a close cousin). The score-difference distribution-matching gradient is what the Proteina-SiD paper adapted to flow matching, so DMD-style is proven feasible on our exact model class.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1-step (and 4-step) generation; DMD2 FID 1.28 on ImageNet 64x64, 8.35 zero-shot COCO; scales to SDXL/megapixel.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High: maintains a generator + a learned fake-score critic (two-network), optional GAN discriminator; needs careful TTUR tuning. But directly analogous to the working protein-SiD recipe.
allDeduped.notes: Important because the ONLY validated Proteina distillation (2510.03095) uses SiD, the sibling of DMD - so DMD/DMD2 represent the proven score-distribution-matching route to few-step Proteina. Heavier (extra critic network) than consistency/shortcut routes. Consider as the alternative distillation backbone if SiD reproduction stalls.

allDeduped.title: DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling in Around 10 Steps
allDeduped.authors: Cheng Lu, Yuhao Zhou, Fan Bao, Jianfei Chen, Chongxuan Li, Jun Zhu
allDeduped.category: training-free fast ODE solver (exponential integrator, high-order)
allDeduped.core_idea: Analytically splits the diffusion/probability-flow ODE into a linear part (solved exactly via an exponential integrator) and a nonlinear part approximated by a high-order (1st/2nd/3rd) Taylor expansion of the network output at a few timesteps. Dedicated high-order solver with convergence-order guarantees; plug-and-play on any pretrained model.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes in principle. The solver is geometry-agnostic (operates on the integrated state) and would run on CA coordinates. Needs the model in noise/x0-prediction form OR the velocity field reparameterized into the exponential-integrator structure; Proteina's velocity (flow-matching) parameterization must be mapped to the semilinear ODE form. Untested on CA-only protein backbones.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 4.70 FID @ NFE=10, 2.87 FID @ NFE=20 on CIFAR-10; high-quality images in 10-20 steps vs 100-250 for DDIM (~10-20x fewer NFE).
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low. Reference PyTorch lib supports both discrete- and continuous-time models; main work is wrapping Proteina's velocity field as a noise/data predictor and supplying the (linear) flow-matching noise schedule.
allDeduped.notes: Foundational training-free solver. Flow-matching's linear/OT path is equivalent (up to schedule rescaling) to a VP diffusion ODE, so DPM-Solver transfers, but the OFFICIAL caveat: it is derived for the score/diffusion semilinear ODE; you must reparameterize the linear FM velocity into that structure. Stochastic SDE term (Proteina default 'sc' sampler) is not handled by the deterministic ODE version (see DPM-Solver++ / SDE-DPM-Solver for that).

allDeduped.title: Fast Sampling of Diffusion Models with Exponential Integrator (DEIS)
allDeduped.authors: Qinsheng Zhang, Yongxin Chen
allDeduped.category: training-free fast ODE solver (exponential integrator, multistep / Adams-type)
allDeduped.core_idea: Exploits the semilinear structure of the probability-flow ODE: computes the linear part exactly via an exponential integrator and fits the nonlinear network term with a multistep (Adams-Bashforth-style) polynomial extrapolation of cached evaluations, minimizing discretization error at small NFE.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes. Geometry-agnostic on the integrated state; requires the semilinear ODE form (reparameterize FM velocity). The multistep/Adams machinery is exactly the structure that the flow-matching-specific solver (2411.07627) re-derives for velocity fields.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: High-quality samples in ~10-15 NFE; reported faster than single-step DPM-Solver at very small NFE due to multistep reuse.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low-medium. Available as DEISMultistepScheduler in diffusers; conceptually close to DPM-Solver++, so try whichever is already wired into your sampler.
allDeduped.notes: Same semilinear-ODE family as DPM-Solver. The polynomial/Adams extrapolation idea is what the flow-matching training-free solver (Song & Lai 2024) adapts directly to velocity-prediction models, so DEIS is the conceptual bridge for FM. tAB (time-Adams-Bashforth) variant matters at NFE<10.

allDeduped.title: Restart Sampling for Improving Generative Processes
allDeduped.authors: Yilun Xu, Mingyang Deng, Xiang Cheng, Yonglong Tian, Ziming Liu, Tommi S. Jaakkola
allDeduped.core_idea: Alternates deterministic ODE steps (low discretization error) with occasional 'restart' steps that re-inject a large burst of noise then re-integrate. The added noise contracts accumulated errors, getting SDE-level quality at ODE-level speed; explicitly balances discretization error vs. contraction.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes, and conceptually well-matched to our setting: Proteina already uses a stochastic ('sc') sampler because pure ODE plateaus; Restart is the principled way to keep noise's quality benefit while spending fewer total NFE. Restart intervals are hyperparameters to tune per model; geometry-agnostic so works on CA coords.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: ~10x speedup on CIFAR-10 and ~2x on ImageNet over previous SDE/ODE samplers at matched quality; beats both ODE and SDE baselines in speed AND accuracy.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium. Need to choose restart positions/intervals and noise levels (tuned per dataset/model); no retraining, but the schedule of restarts requires a small sweep.
allDeduped.notes: Directly addresses the project's core tension (stochastic sampler needed for quality but expensive). Strong candidate for keeping Proteina's noise injection while cutting NFE. Restart hyperparameters are model-specific — would need a small grid search on our CA pipeline (flag as ungrounded until validated).

allDeduped.title: Score Distillation of Flow Matching Models (SiD-DiT)
allDeduped.authors: Gu, Zhou et al. (Apple, UT Austin)
allDeduped.core_idea: The methodological foundation underneath the Proteina-distillation paper: extends Score identity Distillation (SiD) to flow-matching/DiT models for one- and few-step generation, with flow-matching- and DiT-specific adjustments. Works data-free AND data-aided, WITHOUT teacher finetuning or architectural changes. Validated on SANA, SD3/3.5, FLUX. Because Proteina is a flow-matching DiT, this transfers cleanly (and is precisely what the protein paper did). Useful if we want to re-derive/implement the distillation ourselves rather than wait for protein-paper code.
allDeduped.category: Distillation method (SiD for flow matching) - general
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Indirect - image/DiT validation, but flow-matching-DiT-native, so the math carries to Proteina's CA model (demonstrated by the protein distillation paper).
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1- to few-step (NFE 1-4 typical for SiD); large speedups on T2I. Protein realization landed at 16 NFE.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (distillation training), but no teacher finetuning/arch change needed - lowest-overhead distillation family.
allDeduped.notes: Reference implementation/theory for the SiD-on-flow-matching approach we'd port to CA-only.

allDeduped.title: Efficient Molecular Conformer Generation with SO(3)-Averaged Flow Matching and Reflow
allDeduped.authors: Cao, Geiger, dos Santos Costa, Reidenbach, Kreis, Geffner, Pellegrini, Zhou, Kucukbenli (NVIDIA/Stability)
allDeduped.core_idea: Few-step flow matching on 3D MOLECULAR Cartesian coordinates (N x 3, i.e. point-cloud-like, same data geometry as CA-only). Combines an SO(3)-averaged FM training objective with REFLOW + distillation to straighten trajectories and enable 2-step and even 1-step generation. One-step student outperforms 1000-step MCF baselines on precision; 21-50x faster sampling. Shares authors with Proteina (NVIDIA), so techniques are likely portable to the same codebase. Reflow is a strong alternative/complement to SiD: finetune teacher on self-generated noise->sample pairs to linearize, then distill.
allDeduped.category: Reflow + distillation for 3D coordinate flow matching
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Strong analog - operates on 3D Cartesian coords like CA-only; same FM framework and overlapping team. Conformers != folds, but geometry transfers.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1-2 NFE; 21-50x speedup vs multistep baselines.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High: reflow finetuning (generate paired data, retrain) + distillation. But reflow is conceptually simpler than SiD.
allDeduped.notes: Suggests reflow as a second distillation route for Proteina; same-org provenance lowers porting risk. Watch that protein one-step likely fails (per the distillation paper) - aim few-step.

allDeduped.title: Noise schedules considered harmful (conceptual framing)
allDeduped.authors: Sander Dieleman
allDeduped.core_idea: Argues the 'noise schedule' is a confusing, partly redundant abstraction: the relative weighting of noise levels is jointly set by FOUR interchangeable choices - the schedule, the model parameterization (x0 vs eps vs v), the loss weighting w(t), and the time-step distribution p(t). Crucially, the optimal weighting for TRAINING differs from that for SAMPLING (training balances learnability+perceptual relevance; sampling balances prediction accuracy + error accumulation). Therefore the inference time/step schedule should be optimized INDEPENDENTLY of the training distribution, not copied from it. For few-step sampling, concentrate steps where prediction error and error-propagation are worst.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes as guidance - diffusion and flow matching are shown to be the same idea up to noise-level weighting/spacing/scaling, so the framing applies directly. The key actionable principle for us: treat Proteina's inference schedule (schedule_mode/schedule_p/g(t)) as a free, separately-optimized knob and tune it for low-NFE sampling quality rather than inheriting the training-time time distribution.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: n/a (conceptual).
allDeduped.implementation_cost: None - it is the mental model that justifies sweeping schedule_p / discretization independently at inference.
allDeduped.notes: Best single conceptual anchor for this whole track: explains WHY a training-free inference schedule sweep (EDM-rho / SD3-shift / AYS) is legitimate and how the knobs interrelate. Authoritative author (DeepMind). Read before designing the Proteina schedule sweep.

allDeduped.title: Few-Step Diffusion Sampling Through Instance-Aware Discretizations (INDIS)
allDeduped.authors: Liangyu Yuan, Ruoyu Wang, Tong Zhao, Dingwen Fu, Mingkun Lei, Beier Zhu, Chi Zhang
allDeduped.core_idea: Argues a globally fixed schedule ignores instance-specific complexity. Learns to ADAPT the timestep allocation per sample, conditioned on the initial noise and the available guidance/condition, via a gradient-based discretization search extended to conditional generation. Adds a lightweight selector with negligible inference overhead.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conditionally relevant. Explicitly demonstrated on flow-matching models (latent image + video FM), matching our FM setting, and is sequence-CONDITIONED-friendly since it conditions the schedule on the guidance — aligns with our sequence-conditioned sampling. However it requires training a per-instance selector, more machinery than a single shared schedule. Worth it only if we observe that a single global schedule leaves quality on the table across diverse sequences; otherwise GITS/OSS/LD3 (one shared schedule) are simpler. CA-coordinate validation needed.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Reports consistent few-step quality gains over fixed-schedule baselines (specific NFE counts not stated in abstract); negligible inference overhead.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Moderate-high. Train an instance/condition-aware selector network in addition to the schedule search; more complex than a static schedule.
allDeduped.notes: Most relevant of the very-recent works because it is explicitly flow-matching AND conditional (matching our sequence-conditioned use). The per-sample adaptivity could help if fold/topology difficulty varies across sequences, but the extra selector-training cost makes it a second-wave option after the simpler shared-schedule methods are tried.

allDeduped.title: Bespoke Non-Stationary (BNS) Solvers for Fast Sampling of Diffusion and Flow Models
allDeduped.authors: Neta Shaul, Uriel Singer, Ricky T. Q. Chen, Matthew Le, Ali Thabet, Albert Pumarola, Yaron Lipman
allDeduped.category: Learned solver + implicit step placement (bespoke, tiny parameter space)
allDeduped.core_idea: A family of non-stationary ODE solvers (provably subsuming standard solvers) whose <200 parameters — which effectively encode WHERE and HOW to step — are optimized per model on a small set, dramatically improving sample approximation (PSNR) over fixed solvers. Two orders of magnitude faster to fit than model distillation; explicitly covers both diffusion and FLOW models.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Promising and natively flow-matching. From the Lipman/Chen flow-matching group, BNS solvers are explicitly designed for flow models, so they fit Proteina's OT-FM ODE directly. The bespoke parameters subsume timestep placement plus solver behavior, fit on a tiny calibration set with the frozen base model — conceptually the FM-native analogue of Differentiable Solver Search. Caveat: tuned for deterministic ODE sampling; our default stochastic SDE sampler may need the SDE/g(t) treatment, and CA-coordinate fitting needs testing.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Large PSNR/quality gains in the low-NFE regime over fixed solvers; <200 parameters; fitting ~100x faster than model distillation.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Moderate. Fit <200 solver parameters on a small set (frozen base model); needs the differentiable bespoke-solver implementation.
allDeduped.public_code_url: Referenced in paper (Meta/FAIR); see flow-matching repos in github.com/dongzhuoyao/awesome-flow-matching
allDeduped.notes: Authoritative flow-matching-native method (same authorship lineage as Flow Matching itself). It blurs the schedule/solver boundary — the learned parameters implicitly place steps. Strong candidate where we are willing to fit a tiny per-model solver; pairs naturally with our OT-FM formulation. Slightly less 'pure schedule search' than GITS/OSS, hence 4.

allDeduped.title: Generative Modelling With Inverse Heat Dissipation
allDeduped.authors: Severi Rissanen, Markus Heinonen, Arno Solin
allDeduped.core_idea: Builds a generative process by stochastically reversing the heat equation, which erases fine-scale (high-frequency) information first, making the coarse-to-fine inductive bias explicit and structural rather than incidental. Spectral analysis in the paper shows standard diffusion has an IMPLICIT coarse-to-fine bias: low spatial frequencies (global shape/color) are determined early/at high effective noise, fine detail later. Demonstrates emergent disentanglement of overall shape vs detail.
allDeduped.category: Spectral / coarse-to-fine analysis of noise levels
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conceptually relevant (establishes that global shape is decided first), but the specific heat-equation forward process is defined on a 2D image grid; adapting blurring to a 1D CA chain is non-trivial and would require retraining. Use as evidence, not a recipe.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Not framed as a speedup result
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (different forward process; needs retraining; image-grid specific)
allDeduped.notes: Authoritative early evidence that the coarse(global)-to-fine(local) ordering is intrinsic to diffusion and tied to the high-noise regime. Strong citation for the 'fold set first' claim; not directly deployable on our model.

allDeduped.title: A Fourier Space Perspective on Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Fabian Falck, Teodora Pandeva, Kiarash Zahirnia, Rachel Lawrence, Richard Turner, Edward Meeds, Javier Zazo, Sushrut Karmalkar
allDeduped.core_idea: Formalizes per-frequency SNR for DDPM: SNR_i(t) = (alpha_bar_t * C_i)/(1-alpha_bar_t), where C_i is the signal power at frequency i. Because natural-data power C_i decays with frequency, high frequencies hit low SNR much earlier, so the forward process corrupts (and the reverse process generates) low frequencies first and high frequencies last, conditional on the coarse content. Proposes EqualSNR (colored noise giving every frequency the same SNR trajectory) and shows it matches DDPM FID while improving high-frequency realism — i.e. the standard low-to-high ordering starves fine detail of steps.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Concept (per-frequency SNR governs which scale is resolved when) transfers; the EqualSNR fix requires a colored-noise forward process and retraining, and is image-spectrum specific. For us the useful takeaway is the SNR-per-scale framing to reason about which Euler steps lock in the fold.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Not a speedup paper (FID-parity with redistributed effort)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (new forward process + retraining)
allDeduped.notes: Best rigorous treatment of WHY global structure is set at high noise (per-frequency SNR). Important nuance for us: the coarse-first ordering is a property of white noise + power-law data, and is editable.

allDeduped.title: Diffusion is not necessarily Spectral Autoregression (EqualSNR rebuttal)
allDeduped.authors: Fabian Falck
allDeduped.core_idea: Direct rebuttal to Dieleman: the coarse-to-fine / spectral-autoregression behavior is NOT a necessity of diffusion but an implicit CHOICE arising from white noise on power-law data. With EqualSNR (colored noise) all frequencies degrade together and quality is preserved, so the global-then-local ordering can be removed. Implication: where the model 'does its work' across noise levels is a design decision, not a law — which both warns against over-committing to a fixed coarse-first schedule and opens the door to deliberately re-allocating effort.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conceptual caution rather than a method. Tempers the 'fold is always decided at high noise' assumption: for our model that ordering is whatever our (white-noise, OT-FM) forward process induces, and is in principle reshapeable.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: FID-parity, no speedup claim
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High if adopting EqualSNR; low as a conceptual caveat
