allDeduped.notes: Essential balance to Dieleman. For our literature judgment: do NOT treat coarse-to-fine as a guaranteed free lunch for step-skipping; verify empirically where OUR flow-matching model decides topology before reallocating NFE.

allDeduped.title: Low-Pass Flow Matching
allDeduped.authors: Francesco M. Ruscio, T. Konstantin Rusch
allDeduped.core_idea: Flow-matching-specific spectral analysis. Notes standard FM injects white noise (flat spectrum) while data has power decaying as ~1/f^gamma. Replaces scalar noise scaling in the interpolant x_t = t*x1 + L_t*x0 with a time-dependent linear shift-invariant (low-pass) operator L_t whose spectral bias evolves along the path: coarse structure emerges early, fine detail late. Reports improved/maintained quality with substantially fewer NFE under adaptive ODE solvers (up to 2.16x NFE reduction on Galaxy10).
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Most architecturally aligned of the spectral works (same OT-FM interpolant family as Proteina). But L_t is a frequency-domain low-pass operator natural on grids; defining it on a 1D CA chain (e.g. via graph/spectral Laplacian or sequence-position FFT) is an open design step, and it requires retraining with the modified interpolant — not training-free.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Up to 2.16x NFE reduction (Galaxy10, adaptive solver)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (modified interpolant + retraining; CA-chain operator design needed)
allDeduped.notes: Directly flow-matching and directly about NFE reduction via frequency-aware noise — the closest paper to our exact setting on this angle. Worth deep reading; main blocker is it needs retraining and a sensible low-pass operator on protein chains.

allDeduped.title: Spectrally-Guided Diffusion Noise Schedules
allDeduped.authors: Carlos Esteves, Ameesh Makadia
allDeduped.core_idea: Uses each instance's Radially-Averaged Power Spectral Density to derive principled min/max noise levels (from Nyquist/highest-frequency and lowest-frequency content) and interpolate in between, then offers a 'power-focused' schedule that allocates more steps to coarse structure (where signal power concentrates) vs a 'frequency-focused' one for detail, plus a mixed schedule. Operationalizes 'spend steps where the work is' directly as a schedule.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: The recipe (compute a power spectrum, place more noise levels where coarse/global power lives) is adaptable: replace image RAPSD with a power spectrum of CA coordinates along the chain. Schedule is applied at inference, so it can be training-free on top of an existing model. Image-specific assumptions (2D Nyquist) need re-derivation for 1D chains.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Comparable quality with 50% fewer steps (256 vs 512 at 256x256); >3x faster inference at optimal step counts
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium (port spectral-density estimation + schedule construction to CA coordinates; inference-only)
allDeduped.notes: Concrete, training-free-leaning instantiation of the angle's thesis with real step/speedup numbers. Strong template for a protein-spectrum-guided non-uniform schedule that front-loads steps onto the global/coarse regime.

allDeduped.title: Improving diffusion-based protein backbone generation with global-geometry-aware latent encoding (TopoDiff)
allDeduped.authors: Yuyang Zhang et al.
allDeduped.core_idea: Protein-specific evidence that GLOBAL geometry (overall fold/topology, arrangement of secondary-structure elements, radius of gyration) is a distinct factor from residue-level local geometry and is hard to control with bottom-up residue-level diffusion alone. Introduces a 32-dim latent that encodes high-level global geometry; conditioning the residue-level backbone diffusion on it improves coverage of natural fold modes and enables top-down control of overall shape. Demonstrates global topology must be prescribed separately from local detail.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Directly about CA-level protein backbone generation. Strongest protein-domain support that fold/topology is a separable, dominant degree of freedom — consistent with the idea that the global fold is decided early (high noise) while local geometry is refined late, motivating step concentration in the topology-setting regime. The latent-conditioning method itself needs training and is orthogonal to NFE reduction.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Not a speedup result
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (latent encoder + conditioned decoder training); used here as evidence, not as a speed method
allDeduped.notes: Key protein-domain anchor for the angle: confirms global topology is a distinct, hard, high-level factor in CA backbone generation (as in our Proteina setting), supporting the premise that topology-deciding noise levels deserve the step budget. Note ambiguity: 'TopoDiff' is also an unrelated topology-optimization diffusion paper — this is the protein one.

allDeduped.title: Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking in Generative Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Gabriel Raya, Luca Ambrogioni
allDeduped.core_idea: Shows the generative SDE has two phases separated by a spontaneous-symmetry-breaking instability: an early linear/steady-state dynamics around a central fixed point (which contributes little to the final sample and mainly amplifies initial-noise asymmetry into a mode choice) and a late attractor dynamics toward the data manifold. Proposes a 'Gaussian late initialization' scheme: estimate the mean/covariance of the noise-corrupted data at a late start time and initialize sampling from that Gaussian, skipping the uninformative early window. Reports up to 3x FID improvement on fast (few-step) samplers plus higher diversity.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: The most directly actionable acceleration idea in this track: the early steady-state phase is provably low-information, so a 'late start' from a fitted Gaussian (mean+covariance of CA coordinates at the chosen start time) can remove a chunk of the early NFEs. Concept transfers to flow-matching because OT-FM also has a near-linear early segment; the Gaussian-late-start init is sampler-agnostic. Demonstrated on images; for CA we'd fit a per-length Gaussian (or use the prior) at the start time and verify topology diversity is preserved.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Up to 3x FID improvement on fast samplers (gain concentrated in low-NFE regime); not a fixed NFE count
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low-to-moderate: estimate Gaussian moments of noised CA data at a start time and replace the prior draw; training-free; main work is calibrating the late-start time so we don't clip the topology-commitment window
allDeduped.notes: Bridges critical-window THEORY to a concrete training-free speedup. Combine with Biroli's speciation-time estimate to choose the latest start time that still precedes topology commitment. Public code available.

allDeduped.title: Probing the Latent Hierarchical Structure of Data via Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Antonio Sclocchi, Alessandro Favero, Noam Itzhak Levi, Matthieu Wyart
allDeduped.category: fork/forward-backward used as a measurement tool for hierarchical commitment
allDeduped.core_idea: Follow-up that turns the forward-backward (fork) experiment into a general probe: noising to a level and re-denoising produces correlated CHANGES in the output, and the correlation length of those changes jumps at the same phase-transition time where high-level (class) features flip. Confirms that commitment happens hierarchically and at a sharp noise scale, and shows the branch-from-shared-state experiment measures the latent compositional structure of the data.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Provides a quantitative readout (spatial/structural correlation of changes under re-denoising) that could be applied to CA backbones to localize the fold-commitment t without needing an external fold classifier — measure how correlated the topology changes are as a function of branch time.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (probe)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low-medium
allDeduped.notes: Strengthens and generalizes 2402.16991. Same diffusion caveat re: flow matching; protocol still sampler-agnostic.

allDeduped.title: Measuring Semantic Information Production in Generative Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Luca Ambrogioni et al. (Ambrogioni group)
allDeduped.core_idea: Information-theoretic method to measure WHEN class-semantic 'decisions' are made during reverse diffusion. Uses an online optimal-Bayesian-classifier formula to estimate conditional entropy of the class label given the noisy state, then takes its time-derivative (entropy rate) to find intervals of highest information transfer between noisy state and class. Finds semantic information transfer peaks in INTERMEDIATE noise stages and vanishes at the end; different classes have different entropy-rate profiles (decisions located at different times). Demonstrated on 1D GMM and DDPM/CIFAR10.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: High: same template as the Entropic-Signature paper (precursor). The entropy-RATE peak pinpoints the commitment window; with a CA fold classifier this directly yields the fold-commitment noise level for Proteina. Class-specific profiles imply different folds/topologies may commit at different t*, important for our scheduling.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (measurement; class-specific commitment times reported qualitatively)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low-medium: classifier + entropy-rate estimation over noise levels.
allDeduped.notes: Earlier/companion to 2602.09651. Key takeaway for us: commitment is at INTERMEDIATE noise and is class-dependent — so a fold-aware step schedule (dense near the fold's own t*) may beat a single global schedule.

allDeduped.title: De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion (partial diffusion / partial_T)
allDeduped.authors: Joseph L. Watson, David Juergens, Nathaniel R. Bennett, et al. (Baker lab)
allDeduped.core_idea: Establishes empirically (via the 'partial diffusion' / partial_T procedure) that the OVERALL FOLD of a protein backbone is set at the early (low-step-index, lower-noise) part of the trajectory: noising an input structure only 5-25 of 50 steps and re-denoising perturbs local detail while PRESERVING the base fold; more noising steps = more topological diversity. This is direct experimental evidence in a protein backbone diffusion model that fold/topology commitment lives at a specific noise level — beyond it the fold is no longer preserved.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: High (empirical, protein backbone, frame-based; near-CA): demonstrates the existence of a fold-commitment noise level in a real protein generator and a knob (partial_T) that probes it. Method is for SE(3) score-based diffusion on residue frames, not flow-matching, but the partial-noising-to-find-commitment protocol transfers directly to Proteina's SDE (partial-noise + re-integrate) to empirically locate fold commitment.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: partial_T uses only a FRACTION of the 50 steps (e.g. 5-25), i.e. an implicit large NFE reduction when the fold is already fixed — directly analogous to our goal.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low: Proteina already supports partial noise injection (SDE 'sc' sampler); sweep the noise level at which fold is/ isn't preserved using a CA fold classifier / TM-align.
allDeduped.notes: Best PROTEIN-SPECIFIC empirical anchor for 'fold commitment noise level'. The partial_T sweep is the experimental template to run on Proteina: partially noise a sample to level t, re-sample, measure fold retention (TM-align to original / CATH-T label) to find t*.

allDeduped.title: Protein Representation Learning by Geometric Structure Pretraining (GearNet)
allDeduped.authors: Zuobai Zhang, Minghao Xu, Arian Jamasb, Vijil Chenthamarakshan, Aurélie Lozano, Payel Das, Jian Tang
allDeduped.core_idea: Geometry-aware relational GNN over residue (CA-node) graphs that achieves strong protein FOLD CLASSIFICATION (large gains on the Fold/SCOP benchmark). Provides a structure-only embedding ϕ(x) that captures fold/topology — the standard backbone for a learned fold classifier on CA-level structures. This is the exact encoder Proteina adapts to build its GearNet-based CATH (C/A/T) fold classifier for FPSD/fJSD metrics.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: High: operates on residue graphs with CA nodes, so it works on CA-only generated structures. It is the tool to CLASSIFY fold/topology from partial or final CA structures — the front-end for any commitment-window measurement on Proteina. Needs (one-time) training/finetuning to predict CATH topology, which Proteina already did.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (encoder/classifier, not a sampler)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium: a fold classifier must be trained/finetuned once; Proteina's pretrained GearNet-fold classifier can likely be reused directly.
allDeduped.notes: The classifier component of the commitment-detection pipeline (Entropic-Signature / Information-Production methods need exactly such a classifier). Reuse Proteina's GearNet CATH classifier to label partially-denoised CA samples; robustness of GearNet on NOISY/partial CA structures should be checked.

allDeduped.title: US-align: universal structure alignments of proteins, nucleic acids, and macromolecular complexes
allDeduped.authors: Chengxin Zhang, Morgan Shine, Anna Marie Pyle, Yang Zhang
allDeduped.core_idea: Universal TM-score-based structural alignment (extension of TM-align). Provides the TM-score metric whose canonical thresholds (TM>0.5 = same fold in SCOP/CATH; <0.2 = random) make it the standard ALIGNMENT-BASED fold/topology classifier: assign a partial or final structure to a CATH topology by aligning to cluster representatives. CA-only compatible.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: High: TM-align/US-align run on CA traces and yield a calibrated same-fold decision (TM>0.5), giving a training-free alternative to GearNet for labeling fold/topology of partially-denoised CA samples and measuring when the trajectory commits to a CATH-representative fold.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (alignment tool)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low: run TM-align/US-align of each partial sample against CATH topology representatives; threshold TM>0.5 for commitment.
allDeduped.notes: The training-free fold-commitment classifier. For each noise level, align partial sample to the eventual fold's CATH representative; the noise level where TM crosses 0.5 (and stops changing) operationalizes the commitment window without any learned model. Complements GearNet.

allDeduped.title: Flow Map Matching with Stochastic Interpolants: A Mathematical Framework for Consistency Models
allDeduped.authors: Nicholas M. Boffi, Michael S. Albergo, Eric Vanden-Eijnden
allDeduped.core_idea: Provides the mathematical foundation for learning the two-time flow map X_{s,t} of a generative dynamical model under the stochastic-interpolant / flow-matching framework. Shows consistency models, CTM, progressive distillation, and neural-operator methods are special cases.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Indirect/foundational. The stochastic-interpolant view matches flow matching exactly, so it underpins any few-step adaptation of Proteina, but it is a framework, not a turnkey method.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Demonstrates one-/few-step sampling on standard benchmarks; primary contribution is theory unifying few-step methods rather than a speedup record.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium (theory): use it to design the objective; concrete training still required. Superseded for practice by the 2505.18825 self-distillation follow-up.
allDeduped.notes: Read this to understand WHICH few-step method maps onto Proteina's FM/SDE setup and why; it is the unifying lens (FM-native) connecting all the consistency/flow-map entries here. Use the 2025 self-distillation paper for the actual algorithm.

allDeduped.title: Inductive Moment Matching (IMM)
allDeduped.authors: Linqi Zhou, Stefano Ermon, Jiaming Song
allDeduped.category: training-based few-step (moment matching, single-stage, from scratch)
allDeduped.core_idea: A single-stage few-step generative model trained by matching distributions at the moment level along the interpolant, with distribution-level convergence guarantees. Unlike distillation, needs no pretrained teacher or two-network setup; unlike consistency models, stable across hyperparameters.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Plausible. Built on an interpolant/diffusion process compatible with flow matching; would need a from-scratch training run on protein data. No protein results.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 8-step FID 1.99 on ImageNet 256x256 (beats diffusion); 2-step FID 1.98 on CIFAR-10 from scratch.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium-High: from-scratch single-stage training with the moment-matching objective; more stable than CM but still a full training run.
allDeduped.notes: Attractive for stability (distribution-level convergence, robust to hyperparameters) and no teacher, but requires training from scratch and is unproven on geometric/protein data. Lower priority than distilling our existing Proteina checkpoint.

allDeduped.title: Consistency Models
allDeduped.authors: Yang Song, Prafulla Dhariwal, Mark Chen, Ilya Sutskever
allDeduped.core_idea: Learns a consistency function mapping any point on a PF-ODE trajectory directly to its origin, enforcing that all points on a trajectory map to the same output. Two modes: consistency distillation (from a pretrained diffusion/score teacher) or consistency training (from scratch). Enables 1-2 step sampling.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Indirect. Formulated for score/diffusion PF-ODE (EDM); needs adaptation to Proteina's flow-matching ODE (use the FM/flow-map reformulations above instead of the raw method). CA-only is fine in principle.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1-step and 2-step generation; SOTA at the time on CIFAR-10/ImageNet 64; trades steps for quality.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium, but assumes a score/EDM parameterization; mapping to FM requires the score<->velocity conversion (as the protein-SiD paper did).
allDeduped.notes: Seminal reference that everything here builds on. Not directly drop-in for flow matching (diffusion/EDM assumption) - prefer the FM-native descendants (Consistency-FM, Shortcut, flow-map self-distillation) for Proteina. Useful conceptual baseline. (See also sCM / TrigFlow continuous-time CMs, 2024.)

allDeduped.title: Consistency Trajectory Models (CTM)
allDeduped.authors: Dongjun Kim, Chieh-Hsin Lai, Wei-Hsiang Liao, Naoki Murata, Yuhta Takida, Toshimitsu Uesaka, Yutong He, Yuki Mitsufuji, Stefano Ermon
allDeduped.core_idea: Generalizes consistency + score models: a single network predicts anytime-to-anytime jumps along the PF-ODE (from infinitesimal score to long integrals), giving a smooth quality/speed trade-off and access to the score. Trained by distillation with a soft-consistency + adversarial/DSM mix.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Indirect. Built on diffusion PF-ODE; the anytime-jump idea is exactly what we want but needs FM adaptation (subsumed by flow-map matching theory). CA-only compatible in principle.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: SOTA 1-step FID 1.73 (CIFAR-10) and 1.92 (ImageNet 64) at publication; improves monotonically with more steps (unlike CM).
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High: distillation from a diffusion teacher plus adversarial/DSM terms; assumes score parameterization.
allDeduped.notes: Key precursor to flow-map methods; its anytime-to-anytime jump is the conceptual core of tunable-NFE sampling for our pipeline. Diffusion-formulated, so use the FM flow-map reformulations to instantiate on Proteina. Avoids CM's quality degradation at higher steps.

allDeduped.title: DPM-Solver-v3: Improved Diffusion ODE Solver with Empirical Model Statistics
allDeduped.authors: Kaiwen Zheng, Cheng Lu, Jianfei Chen, Jun Zhu
allDeduped.category: training-free fast ODE solver (multistep + predictor-corrector + empirical statistics)
allDeduped.core_idea: Improves the DPM-Solver family by computing 'empirical model statistics' (EMS) — coefficients estimated from a few forward passes of the pretrained model — to choose an optimal parameterization that minimizes the first-order discretization error, combined with multistep and predictor-corrector. Squeezes extra quality at the very lowest NFE.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Yes, with the same FM reparameterization as DPM-Solver. The EMS precomputation is a small offline pass over the frozen Proteina model (no training); benefit is concentrated at NFE 5-10. Untested on CA structures.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 15-30% lower FID than DPM-Solver++/UniPC at NFE=5-10 (e.g. notable gains in the 5-10 NFE regime on CIFAR-10/ImageNet/Stable Diffusion).
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Medium. Needs a one-time EMS computation per model/schedule; more moving parts than DPM-Solver++ for a modest extra gain.
allDeduped.notes: Worth trying only if DPM-Solver++/UniPC plateau and we need the last bit of quality at NFE<=10. Extra EMS precompute step adds friction; deterministic-ODE only (no stochastic term). Lower priority than UniPC/EDM/AYS for a first pass.

allDeduped.title: Few-step Flow for 3D Generation via Marginal-Data Transport Distillation (MDT-dist)
allDeduped.authors: TRELLIS-based 3D generation team
allDeduped.core_idea: Few-step distillation specifically for FLOW-matching 3D generative models. Distills the pretrained flow to learn the Marginal-Data Transport via two objectives - Velocity Matching and Velocity Distillation - moving the target from transport level to velocity/distribution level. On TRELLIS 3D it cuts 25 steps to 1-2 while preserving geometric fidelity. Demonstrates few-step distillation is viable for 3D structured outputs (not just images), reinforcing feasibility for protein 3D backbones. 3D here = latent/voxel-ish assets, not point clouds, so geometry mismatch is larger than the molecular-conformer paper.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Weak-moderate - 3D but different representation (TRELLIS latents), not CA coordinates. Transfer is conceptual.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: 1-2 NFE (from 25); large speedup.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (distillation training); objectives are flow-matching-specific and reusable.
allDeduped.notes: Evidence that FM distillation generalizes to 3D; Velocity-Matching/Distillation objectives are a candidate recipe variant.

allDeduped.title: FrameFlow / FrameDiff: Fast protein backbone generation with SE(3) flow matching
allDeduped.authors: Yim, Campbell, Mathieu, Foong, Gao, Jaakkola, Barzilay et al. (MIT, Microsoft, Genentech)
allDeduped.core_idea: Same architecture as the diffusion model FrameDiff but trained with SE(3) FLOW MATCHING instead of diffusion; achieves comparable/better designability with ~5x FEWER sampling timesteps (~100 vs ~500). Establishes the core empirical fact that flow matching alone buys a large step reduction over diffusion for protein backbones - the rationale Proteina builds on. Useful as evidence/anchor for how low fixed-step Euler can go on SE(3)/backbone before any distillation, and the FrameFlow/FrameDiff codebase is a reference for SE(3) FM sampling schedules.
allDeduped.category: Flow matching vs diffusion step-reduction (protein backbone)
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Direct domain (protein backbone, SE(3) frames incl. CA translations). Architecturally different from Proteina but same problem.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: ~100 steps (5x fewer than FrameDiff's ~500); 2x better designability.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: N/A as accelerator (it's a model); informative for choosing FM step schedules.
allDeduped.notes: Context/justification more than a drop-in method: shows FM->fewer steps is real for backbones; helps calibrate how aggressive to be with Euler step cuts.

allDeduped.title: On the Importance of Noise Scheduling for Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Ting Chen
allDeduped.category: noise schedule vs data dimensionality / input scaling
allDeduped.core_idea: Empirical study with three findings: (1) noise scheduling is crucial and the optimum is task-dependent; (2) as image size grows, the optimal schedule shifts NOISIER because higher-dimensional data is more redundant (easier to denoise per pixel); (3) simply scaling input data by factor b (equivalent to shifting logSNR by log b) while keeping the schedule fixed is a robust way to adapt across sizes. Frames schedule choice as a function of effective signal redundancy/dimensionality.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conceptually yes, with caveats - the key transferable insight is that the optimal schedule depends on the effective dimensionality/redundancy of the data, which for CA backbones scales with sequence length. Suggests the optimal Proteina schedule may be length-dependent (longer chains -> shift toward noisier/earlier emphasis), echoing the SD3 resolution->shift scaling. The input-scaling trick itself is a training choice (logSNR shift) and is more naturally a diffusion concept than a clean FM lever.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: No NFE-reduction headline (it is a training-quality study, not a few-step-sampling study); enables single-stage 1024px generation.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: The schedule/logSNR-shift insight is conceptual guidance; acting on it (input scaling) requires retraining, so low immediate value for training-free NFE cuts.
allDeduped.notes: Useful as the theoretical 'why' behind length/resolution-dependent shifts (informs whether Proteina's schedule should vary with backbone length), but it is primarily about TRAINING-time scheduling, not inference NFE reduction. Include for conceptual grounding, not as a direct method.

allDeduped.title: Improved Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (cosine noise schedule)
allDeduped.authors: Alex Nichol, Prafulla Dhariwal
allDeduped.core_idea: Introduces the cosine alpha-bar schedule as a fix to the linear schedule, whose final steps are nearly pure noise and thus wasted. Cosine adds noise more gradually, spending more capacity on the perceptually informative mid-range and improving log-likelihood and sample quality. Origin of the now-standard cosine/SNR schedule that later FM/diffusion works (cosmap, SD3) build on.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Indirectly - the cosine schedule is the historical root of the cos_sch_v_snr and loglinear modes Proteina already exposes; the SNR/cosine framing translates to a specific FM time-warp (cosmap). As a sampling-time time-warp it is training-free; as originally posed (a training noise schedule) it is a training choice. Worth including the cosine/cosmap warp in the schedule sweep alongside power/edm.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: No few-step NFE headline; improves NLL/FID at standard step counts.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Trivial as a time-warp (already a Proteina schedule_mode option).
allDeduped.notes: Foundational and already represented among Proteina's schedule_mode choices (cos_sch_v_snr). Include cosmap/cosine in the sweep, but it is unlikely to be the single best few-step lever on its own - EDM-rho / SD3-shift / AYS are more targeted at the low-NFE regime.

allDeduped.title: simple diffusion: End-to-end diffusion for high resolution images (shifted noise schedule)
allDeduped.authors: Emiel Hoogeboom, Jonathan Heek, Tim Salimans
allDeduped.core_idea: Argues high-resolution signals need MORE noise to be destroyed, so the noise schedule should be shifted toward lower SNR as resolution increases (a logSNR shift of -log(resolution-ratio)). Formalizes the dimensionality->schedule-shift relationship that SD3's resolution shift and Ting Chen's input scaling both express. Shift is applied at low SNR, not high.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conceptually yes - same dimensionality-driven-shift principle that should make Proteina's optimal schedule depend on backbone length (more residues -> shift). It is the diffusion-SNR formulation; the clean FM analog is the SD3 time-shift. Primarily a training-time prescription, so direct training-free use is limited.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Not an NFE-reduction paper.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low as a logSNR/time shift, but framed as a training schedule.
allDeduped.notes: Reinforces (with Ting Chen and SD3) that schedule shift should scale with effective data dimension/length. Use as theory for a length-conditioned shift on Proteina; the actionable training-free version is the SD3 shift, so this is supporting evidence rather than a separate method.

allDeduped.title: Dense-Jump Flow Matching with Non-Uniform Time Scheduling (Beta-distribution sampling + terminal jump)
allDeduped.authors: Zidong Chen, Zihao Guo, Peng Wang, ThankGod Itua Egbe, Yan Lyu, Chenghao Qian
allDeduped.category: flow-matching non-uniform time schedule + late-time jump
allDeduped.core_idea: Two parts. (1) TRAINING: sample time t ~ Beta(alpha,alpha), alpha<1, a U-shaped density concentrating mass near both t=0 and t=1 (anchors conditioning early, regularizes the velocity field to lower curvature near t=1). (2) INFERENCE (training-free-ish): 'dense-jump' integration - (N-1) uniform steps over a stable interval [0,t_jump] then ONE direct jump to t=1, avoiding the late-time region where the velocity Lipschitz constant diverges. Stabilizes/avoids the degradation that otherwise grows with more steps.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Partially - it is genuinely flow-matching and ODE-based, so the dense-jump inference idea (skip the unstable late-time stretch with a terminal jump) is a transferable, low-cost trick to try on Proteina's Euler sampler; the Beta-time training part needs retraining. Demonstrated only on robotic-policy action generation, so the late-time-instability premise must be verified for CA backbones before relying on it.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Flexible across 1-64 steps; up to +23.7% task performance over baselines and removes the more-steps-hurts degradation typical of vanilla FM.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low for the inference dense-jump (modify the time grid + one terminal step); the Beta training schedule needs retraining.
allDeduped.notes: Domain is robotics, not proteins, so lower confidence, but it is one of the few native-FM works that explicitly engineers the inference TIME schedule (terminal jump) for few-step stability - conceptually complementary to EDM-rho/SD3-shift. Worth a quick training-free trial of the terminal-jump idea.

allDeduped.title: Accelerating Diffusion Sampling with Optimized Time Steps (DPM-OS)
allDeduped.authors: Shuchen Xue, Zhaoqiang Liu, Fei Chen, Shifeng Zhang, Tianyang Hu, Enze Xie, Zhenguo Li
allDeduped.category: Optimization-based time-step search for a given high-order solver
allDeduped.core_idea: Sets up an explicit optimization problem that chooses time steps to minimize the distance between the ground-truth ODE solution and the numerical solver's approximation for a SPECIFIC solver (e.g. UniPC/DPM-Solver++). Solved with a constrained trust-region method in under 15 seconds. No reference-trajectory distillation needed — uses the solver's truncation-error structure directly.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Partial. The optimization is derived from the semi-linear structure and exponential-integrator error terms of DPM-Solver/UniPC on the diffusion (VP/EDM) ODE, so the exact objective is tied to those solvers and the diffusion parameterization. For our linear-OT flow-matching Euler sampler the specific error expansion differs and would need re-derivation; the high-level 'solve a tiny optimization for time steps that minimize solver truncation error' idea is reusable. Most directly useful if we adopt a higher-order exponential solver for FM.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Few-step regime (≈5-10 steps); significant FID improvements over uniform/logSNR steps on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with UniPC; optimization itself <15s.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low to reuse with the same solver family; moderate re-derivation for an FM-specific solver.
allDeduped.notes: Authoritative, fast, training-free, but the objective is welded to DPM-Solver/UniPC-style exponential integrators on the diffusion ODE. Less plug-and-play for a plain FM Euler/SDE sampler than GITS/OSS/LD3, but worth citing as the optimization-based alternative and relevant if we switch solver families.

allDeduped.title: Entropic Time Schedulers for Generative Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Dejan Stancevic, Florian Handke, Luca Ambrogioni
allDeduped.core_idea: Chooses sampling times so each step contributes an EQUAL amount of information (entropy) to the final sample, rather than uniform spacing. Derives an analytical formula for the optimal 'entropic time' schedule (plus a rescaled variant), estimating entropic time from the training loss / model outputs using only quantities available at inference. For the EDM noise schedule there is a closed-form optimal schedule.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Partial-to-promising. The entropic-time principle (equal information per step) is general, but the analytical schedule and the entropy estimator are derived for the diffusion forward process / EDM noise schedule; mapping 'information content' onto a linear-OT flow-matching interpolant requires re-deriving the entropy-vs-t relation for the FM corruption. If derivable, it would give a cheap, principled, fully analytical non-uniform schedule with no optimization at all — attractive. Uses only inference-time quantities, so no model changes.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Strong gains in the few-NFE regime (improved FID and FD-DINO on EDM2 without increasing NFE); no added function evaluations.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low. Analytical/estimator-based reschedule; no optimization loop and no training.
allDeduped.public_code_url: GitHub link provided in paper (Radboud/Ambrogioni group)
allDeduped.notes: Elegant training-free analytical alternative to the DP/optimization methods. Tied to the diffusion/EDM entropy derivation, so transfer to OT flow-matching needs a re-derivation of information-per-step, but conceptually clean and cheap. Good complementary citation for the 'principled non-uniform schedule' framing.

allDeduped.title: Shaping Inductive Bias in Diffusion Models through Frequency-Based Noise Control
allDeduped.authors: Thomas Jiralerspong, Berton Earnshaw, Jason Hartford, Yoshua Bengio, Luca Scimeca
allDeduped.core_idea: Shows that manipulating the forward noising operator in Fourier space (e.g. colored noise with more power on low frequencies) steers the model to focus on particular frequency bands / aspects of the distribution. Reinforces that global (low-frequency) vs detail (high-frequency) learning is controllable through where noise power is placed, supporting frequency-aware rather than uniform schedules.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conceptual support that emphasizing low frequencies focuses the model on global structure. Method modifies the forward process (needs retraining) and is image-framed; for us it is evidence for, not a drop-in recipe.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None (inductive-bias study)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High (modified forward process + retraining)
allDeduped.notes: Good corroborating evidence that low-frequency = global is steerable, but workshop-scale and not efficiency-focused; lower priority than Dieleman/Falck/Esteves for our purposes.

allDeduped.title: Stage-wise Dynamics of Classifier-Free Guidance in Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: (Anonymous/preprint authors), arXiv 2509.22007
allDeduped.core_idea: Empirically decomposes guided sampling into three successive stages - Direction Shift (move toward weighted mean), Mode Separation (suppress weaker modes / commit to a mode), and Concentration (within-mode contraction). Shows early strong guidance erodes global diversity while late strong guidance only suppresses fine variation, and that time-varying (stage-aware) guidance schedules improve both quality and diversity.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Relevant to our SEQUENCE-conditioned setting: it says the mode-commitment (= topology selection) stage is early and distinct from the late refinement stage, so conditioning/guidance strength - and by extension step density - should be front-loaded around the commitment stage and relaxed afterward. Stage decomposition is sampler-agnostic and applies to our conditional flow-matching sampler; but it is about WHERE conditioning matters, not directly an NFE-cut, and is demonstrated only on image CFG.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None (schedule-quality study, not speed)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low-to-moderate: motivates a stage-aware guidance/step schedule we can ablate on Proteina; no retraining
allDeduped.notes: Most useful for tuning WHEN sequence-conditioning/guidance is applied so we can safely coarsen post-commitment steps; complements the critical-window papers on the conditional side.

allDeduped.title: Perception Prioritized Training of Diffusion Models (P2 weighting)
allDeduped.authors: Jooyoung Choi, Jungbeom Lee, Chaehun Shin, Sungwon Kim, Hyunwoo Kim, Sungroh Yoon
allDeduped.category: SNR window where 'content'/category is decided (empirical localization)
allDeduped.core_idea: Empirically partitions the noise axis into three stages by signal-to-noise ratio: 'coarse' (SNR < 1e-2, global color/layout), 'CONTENT' (1e-2 < SNR < 1e0, where the perceptually meaningful object/category is formed), and 'clean-up' (SNR > 1e0, imperceptible detail). This pins the SNR band in which the category is committed. Motivation is a training reweighting (emphasize the content stage), but the diagnostic finding — that category lives in a specific SNR window — is exactly the commitment-window claim, located concretely.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Two uses: (1) the SNR-stage decomposition tells you WHERE to spend NFE (the content/commitment band) vs where to coarsen (clean-up band) — directly relevant to cutting NFE without hurting fold quality, and training-free if used only to pick a schedule; (2) the P2 reweighting itself needs retraining. SNR is well-defined for the flow-matching interpolant, so the band concept transfers.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (training-quality method; not an NFE reducer per se)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: low if only used to choose where to concentrate steps; high (full retrain) if applying P2 weighting
allDeduped.notes: Bridges the commitment-window idea to a concrete SNR band. The reweighting requires training; the SNR-stage insight is training-free and usable as a schedule-design heuristic for the existing schedule_mode/schedule_p knobs.

allDeduped.title: Diffusion is spectral autoregression (blog) + Diffusion models are autoencoders (blog)
allDeduped.authors: Sander Dieleman
allDeduped.core_idea: The specific Dieleman target. NOTE: I read Perspectives on diffusion, Noise schedules considered harmful, Diffusion models are autoencoders, Geometry of guidance, Guidance cheat-code, and Spectral autoregression — NONE of them runs the fork/branch-from-noisy-state experiment with cat/dog category-commitment. Dieleman provides the conceptual underpinning, not the experiment: sampling is coarse-to-fine, high noise levels fix LOW spatial frequencies / large-scale structure (i.e. the global content/category) while low noise levels add high-frequency detail; he frames this as 'approximate autoregression in frequency space' and stresses that different noise levels have very different perceptual importance. So the global-content-decided-early intuition is his; the explicit fork experiment is in DDPM / the physics papers above.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Conceptual only. The frequency/coarse-to-fine framing is about images; for CA-only backbones the analog would be coarse global fold vs local geometry, but the spectral-power-law argument does not transfer cleanly to 3D point sets, so use the intuition, not the spectral specifics.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A
allDeduped.implementation_cost: N/A (reading)
allDeduped.notes: Authoritative conceptual reference for WHY category is decided early, but does NOT contain the branch/fork commitment experiment the prompt hoped to find in Dieleman. The experiment itself is DDPM (2006.11239) + Sclocchi-Wyart (2402.16991). Flagging this explicitly so the search isn't mis-attributed.

allDeduped.title: FoldingDiff: Protein structure generation via folding diffusion
allDeduped.authors: Kevin E. Wu, Kevin K. Yang, Rianne van den Berg, Sarah Alamdari, James Y. Zou, Alex X. Lu, Ava P. Amini
allDeduped.core_idea: Backbone-torsion-angle diffusion that iteratively denoises from an unfolded (random-angle) state toward a folded structure. Reports a coarse-to-fine emergence: large-scale fold/secondary-structure elements appear earlier in denoising, with fine angles refined later — an empirical illustration of structure-level ordering (secondary/global before fine) in a protein diffusion model.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Medium: torsion-angle (not CA-coordinate) representation, score-based diffusion — so not a direct method transfer, but provides protein-domain evidence that secondary-structure/fold emerges before fine geometry during denoising, supporting a fold-commitment-then-refine schedule. Use as corroborating evidence, not as a method.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: N/A (illustrates emergence order; no NFE-saving claim)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: N/A (reference/evidence)
allDeduped.notes: Supporting evidence for coarse-to-fine (secondary/global before fine) emergence in proteins. Weaker than RFdiffusion's partial_T result because it is qualitative and in torsion space; include as corroboration of the ordering assumption.

allDeduped.title: The Statistical Thermodynamics of Generative Diffusion Models: Phase Transitions, Symmetry Breaking and Critical Instability
allDeduped.authors: Luca Ambrogioni
allDeduped.core_idea: Equilibrium-statistical-mechanics treatment showing generative diffusion undergoes second-order (mean-field universality) phase transitions corresponding to symmetry breaking, and that the critical instability at these transitions is what enables generative diversity. Provides the free-energy-landscape language (minima = data modes; transitions = mode selection) underlying the speciation picture.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Foundational/explanatory rather than directly usable. Tells us the topology-selection transition is mean-field second-order, hence sharp and well-localized in time - reassuring for the strategy of concentrating steps there. No estimator or sampler; not protein- or flow-matching-specific.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None
allDeduped.implementation_cost: n/a (conceptual)
allDeduped.notes: Background theory that legitimizes the symmetry-breaking framing; cite for completeness, not for implementation.

allDeduped.title: The Information Dynamics of Generative Diffusion
allDeduped.authors: Dejan Stancevic, Luca Ambrogioni
allDeduped.core_idea: Unifies information-theoretic, dynamical and thermodynamic views: the rate of conditional entropy production during generation equals the expected divergence of the score vector field, and generative bifurcations (symmetry-breaking decisions) appear as PEAKS in the variance of pathwise conditional entropy. Gives a per-trajectory observable that pinpoints when individual samples resolve their high-level uncertainty.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Offers a DETECTOR for the commitment moment: a peak in pathwise-entropy variance (or score-divergence) marks where a sample is deciding its fold. In principle computable from Proteina's predicted velocity/score along a trajectory, letting us locate (even adaptively, per-sample) where to spend steps. Score-divergence is defined for diffusion; for a flow-matching velocity field the analogous divergence quantity exists but the entropy-production identity must be re-derived. No code, no protein experiments.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: None (diagnostic, not a sampler)
allDeduped.implementation_cost: moderate-to-high: estimating divergence/entropy-variance along trajectories is feasible but needs derivation for the FM ODE and validation that the peak coincides with topology commitment
allDeduped.notes: Interesting as a per-sample adaptive-step trigger if we want dynamic NFE allocation; speculative for flow matching and unproven on proteins.

allDeduped.title: Progressive Distillation for Fast Sampling of Diffusion Models
allDeduped.authors: Tim Salimans, Jonathan Ho
allDeduped.core_idea: Repeatedly distills a deterministic sampler so a student takes half as many steps as its teacher, applied progressively (e.g. 1024->512->...->4->1). Introduces stable parameterizations (v-prediction) for very few steps.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Indirect but adaptable. The step-halving recipe is solver-agnostic and could be applied to Proteina's deterministic (ODE) sampler, but the v-parameterization assumes diffusion; needs the FM analog. CA-only fine.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Down to 4-8 steps with quality near the many-step teacher; multiple distillation rounds required.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: High in wall-clock: several sequential distillation rounds, each a training run; conceptually simple though.
allDeduped.notes: Foundational but dated and multi-round; later single-stage methods (Shortcut, MeanFlow, flow-map self-distillation) dominate it for our use. Mainly useful as a baseline / for the v-prediction stability insight. Requires a deterministic teacher sampler (our default is stochastic SDE).

allDeduped.title: Efficient protein structure generation with sparse denoising models (salad)
allDeduped.authors: salad authors (see Nature MI 2025)
allDeduped.core_idea: A separate protein backbone generator ('salad') built around sparse denoising for efficiency. Cited as needing ~400 denoising steps by default (comparable to Chroma's 500), but the architectural sparsity reduces per-step cost rather than step count. Included for completeness as a contemporary efficient-protein-generation point of comparison; it is an alternative model, NOT a training-free accelerator for Proteina, so its direct utility to our goal is limited. Confirms the field-wide step-count landscape (RFdiffusion 50, FrameFlow 100, salad/Proteina 400, Chroma 500, Genie2 1000).
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Domain-relevant (backbone generation) but not a method we apply to Proteina; architectural, not a sampler accelerator.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: ~400 steps default; efficiency from per-step sparsity, not fewer steps.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Not applicable - different model.
allDeduped.notes: Lowest direct relevance here; mainly anchors the step-count comparison table across protein generators.

allDeduped.title: Common Diffusion Noise Schedules and Sample Steps are Flawed (zero terminal SNR)
allDeduped.authors: Shanchuan Lin, Bingchen Liu, Jiashi Li, Xiao Yang (ByteDance)
allDeduped.public_code_url: not found (fixes described in paper text)
allDeduped.core_idea: Shows common diffusion schedules do NOT reach zero SNR at the terminal step and some samplers do not start from the true last timestep, creating a train/inference mismatch (model never sees pure noise at train time but is given it at inference). Four fixes: rescale schedule to zero terminal SNR, use v-prediction, force the sampler to start at the last timestep, and rescale CFG. The general lesson: schedule ENDPOINTS and which steps you actually visit matter, not just spacing.
allDeduped.applicable_to_protein_ca: Mostly N/A as a method - flow matching is endpoint-correct by construction (linear OT interpolation starts at pure noise t=1/0 boundary exactly), so the specific zero-terminal-SNR bug does not arise. The transferable lesson is hygiene: verify Proteina's first integration step actually starts from pure prior and the last step lands at clean data, and that no boundary step is silently skipped when reducing NFE.
allDeduped.reported_nfe_or_speedup: Not an NFE-reduction paper; fixes brightness/exposure range, not speed.
allDeduped.implementation_cost: Low (it is a correctness checklist for endpoints), but mostly already satisfied by FM.
allDeduped.notes: Included for completeness of the noise-schedule canon. Diffusion-specific; the only takeaway for our FM/CA setting is to sanity-check schedule endpoints when truncating steps. Not a speed lever.
